f: 2024-03-29_10-44-20 - FW_ _EXTERNAL_ Comments on Alameda Lane Reduction proposal.pdf
s: FW: Comments on Alameda Lane Reduction proposal
to: "Lanphier; Molly - DOTI CE2294 Project Manager II Engineering" <Molly.Lanphier@denvergov.org>;
b:
Sorry my email isn’t as fun to share as Greg’s was
Hadar Albo, PE | Engineer City & County of Denver Department of Transportation & Infrastructure |
Transportation Implementation Division Pronouns<https://www.mypronouns.org/what-and-why> | She/Her/Hers
hadar.albo@denvergov.org<mailto:hadar.albo@denvergov.org>
From: Jill Anschutz <jill.hamilton@mac.com> Sent: Thursday, March 28, 2024 9:53 PM To: Albo, Hadar - DOTI
CE0397 Engineer <Hadar.Albo@denvergov.org> Subject: [EXTERNAL] Comments on Alameda Lane
Reduction proposal
Hello,
I wanted to voice serious concerns my husband and I have about DOTI's proposed lane changes to the
one-mile stretch of Alameda between S Logan and S Franklin. We have lived within a few blocks of Alameda in
this area for over 10 years in several different homes, so we are very familiar with this area both as drivers and
pedestrians. We have flexible work-from-home schedules so we use Alameda in this stretch in both directions
at all times of the day and on all days of the week, including during the busiest hours in the morning commute
and afternoon commute on weekdays.
Clearly, Alameda is a critical East-West corridor for vehicles. As traffic has increased significantly on Speer Blvd
to the north of this stretch of Alameda, and with Wash Park and then I-25 to the south, there is not another
efficient thoroughfare for vehicles needing to travel east or west in this neighborhood. We absolutely must
preserve the flow of traffic in this area for the convenience of residents and for access to local businesses and
one of the city's best parks for others.
While we certainly would not want to see changes that make things less safe for pedestrians - and we are
supportive of the flashing cross walk addition at S Franklin - we feel strongly that choking down on traffic lanes
for vehicles by designating a turn only center lane will have a net negative impact on this part of the city.
We have watched closely as DOTI has reduced traffic lanes in other areas of the city in the name of safety; the
areas where we experience this the most frequently are along S Broadway and also on east-bound 7th Avenue
in the historic district area (near Little Cheeseman). As regular pedestrians and drivers in these areas, we think
reducing lanes by designating a lane for turning only creates confusion for drivers, which makes things much
less safe for pedestrians.
Anyone driving regularly on Alameda has surely experienced traffic backing up while someone waits to turn
across opposing traffic, however even with highly frequent use of this stretch of Alameda, it is infrequent that
this backup becomes so significant that it's notable. In our experience, 4-5 pm is the toughest time of day to turn
and people are traveling slower along Alameda at that time anyway because of the volume of cars. Therefore
we believe designating a turn lane by reducing the other lanes will choke up on the regular flow of traffic,
creating frequent frustration and more regular backups for drivers at all times of day. Yes, it will be easier to
turn, but it should be weighted out the number of drivers turning vs. how many need to drive smoothly, safely
and efficiently along Alameda without stopping or turning in this stretch? That is not a trade off we would ever
want to make.
When I wrote to Councilman Kashmann, I also noted the differences that can already be observed between
one-lane and two-lane sections of Alameda in this part of the city. The one-lane areas experience HUGE and
frustrating backups throughout the day, with it becoming almost unnavigable for drivers during morning and
afternoon rush hours. On the other hand, the two-lane portions of Alameda typically have good flow for vehicles
throughout the day. Perhaps the city would consider installing the flashing crosswalk at S Franklin and leaving it
at that for some time to see how that impacts pedestrian safety before making drastic changes to the lanes in
this same section of Alameda?
We would also be interested in seeing data from DOTI about the impacts the city is seeing on safety in the
areas where vehicle lanes have been reduced and new pedestrian access points have been added. Is the
presentation from the March 20 community meeting available? Our own experiences as drivers and pedestrians
in areas where similar changes have been made by DOTI have not convinced us that these are
"improvements." We think they are confusing and frustrating for drivers and pedestrians and would be very
disappointed to see these "improvements" made along Alameda.
Sincerely,
Jill Anschutz

f: 2025-02-25_14-23-44 - _EXTERNAL_ ALR Call with Dallas Howell (2_25_25) - MEC Notes.pdf

to: "Howell; Dallas - DOTI Project Manager II Engineering" <Dallas.Howell@denvergov.org>; "Boncore; Brett -
cc: "Ciarkowski; Matt" <matt.ciarkowski@kimley-horn.com>
b:
Hi, Dallas. Thanks for taking the time to connect on the phone today regarding the Alameda Lane Reduction
project below. Please find my informal meeting notes below for reference.
* Amendment #2 – Includes 20 hours of effort for Bidding and Construction Phase Services ($3.4K) *
Amendment #3 – Includes final design efforts completed that were out of scope, attached for reference ($11K).
This amendment is good as-is unless there are further substantial changes anticipated by the City. *
@Boncore, Brett - PW CE0397 Engineer<mailto:Brett.Boncore@denvergov.org> – Can you confirm if there are
any final comments on the plans and specifications outstanding? * Construction – Anticipated +/- Q2. *
Amendment – Expect 3-5 weeks for processing.
Let me know if there is anything that you would like to add or clarify. Thanks!

b:

Best- Molly

From: Griggs, Gail - MO MO Constituent Services Specialist <Gail.Griggs@denvergov.org> Sent: Monday,
April 7, 2025 3:45 PM To: Lanphier, Molly - DOTI CE2294 Project Manager II Engineering
<Molly.Lanphier@denvergov.org> Subject: Upcoming Roadway Changes to Alameda: [EXTERNAL] No contact
received
Hello Molly –
Please read below regarding upcoming Roadway Changes to Alameda.
Below is the email that was sent from 311, copied from Salesforce. Dear Mayor Johnston and Staff,
I am writing this email to inform you that *many members of the community are concerned about the proposed
change of Alameda from Logan to Franklin to one lane in each direction with a middle turn lane.*
I attended a community meeting in 2024 regarding this proposed change. There were many members of the
community there. * A lot of people did not think this proposed change was a good idea.* 1. This proposed
roadway change will make traffic on a major road (Alameda) which connects to another major road (University)
even worse and will cause traffic to flow into the west Washington Park neighborhood and also increase traffic
on Logan and Downing (which are already overloaded). 2. This type of change to Alameda (in the same stretch)
had occurred in 2008-2009 but was reverted to the present design. There was too much traffic backing up to
keep the design. When asked how this change is going to be better than the design in 2008...the r*esponse
from Molly Lanphier (DOTI CE 2294 Project Manager) is that there is less traffic on Alameda now than in 2008
and thus makes this a good option. *I find this hard to believe unless the road engineers are using traffic data
from 2020-2022 when there was a pandemic and a lot of people were working from home. Furthermore, a
number of BIG apartment buildings on Alameda and Bannock have been completed so the traffic has increased
since 2008 NOT decreased. 3. It is stated that Alameda (Logan to Franklin) is a high accident area and needs
to be altered for safety. However, when I ask what the metrics for success were, a general response of turning
a four lane road to a two lane road with a turning lane usually will cause a reduction of crashes around 19-47%.
No specific data to this stretch of Alameda was given. 4. I also asked if the grading of intersections for cars
would be considered as a metric. I have become aware that intersections are graded (A-F) based on how many
traffic light cycles need to be waited through before a car can proceed through an intersection. Molly said she
did not know about this metric.
I have lived in Denver since 2008. From 2013 onward the roads have become congested and I would agree
more dangerous. However, a contributing factor is that the *road engineers are not taking into account how to
slow traffic BUT keep it flowing. The fact is that the city is growing and novel ideas should be considered - such
as roundabouts.*
There were supposed to be more community outreach days regarding this proposed change to Alameda but
they were not scheduled. I received a general email about 2 months ago stating the Alameda project was going
ahead. I emailed Molly a couple of times but it seems the decision has been made.
This email is to let the mayor's office know that a lot of people do not want this change to occur. *When the
complaints of how bad traffic on this stretch of Alameda start to come in, you will know the community did not
agree with this change but it was pushed through by the engineers on the project.*
There is a part of me that hopes that you can put a stop to this change but I truly feel it is a done deal. I just
want you all to know that a lot of people in the community do not want this change on Alameda to occur.
Sincerely, Martina Hauptmann
Thank-you.
Best Regards!
Gail Griggs Office of The Mayor| City and County of Denver

f: 2025-04-15_09-26-27 - RE_ Organizing to stop lane reduction on Alameda.pdf
s: RE: Organizing to stop lane reduction on Alameda
cc: "Lanphier; Molly - DOTI CE2294 Project Manager II Engineering" <Molly.Lanphier@denvergov.org>
b:
Alaina,
Good call. The outreach is on the project website, but I am including it here:
* Data Collection: October-December 2019 – Completed * Analysis/ Alternatives Evaluation: January-March
2020 – Completed * Public Outreach: May 2020 – Completed * Design Procurement – August 2021 –
Completed * Design: Fall 2023 – Completed * Public Outreach: March 2024 – Completed * Construction
Advertisement: Winter 2025 * Construction: mid-2025
This project was presented to the community in person on March 20, 2024 and virtually on May 19, 2020.
We heard from both sides of this project, many of those opposed to the project were commuters that drive the
corridor, and many of the residents and businesses along the corridor support the project. Many are concerned
with speeds and crashes that have occurred.
Lastly, we flyered the corridor yesterday (4/14) to provide businesses and residents information on the
upcoming project. We did not hear any negative concerns or comments from those we met with.
Thanks, Greg
Greg Cieciek, PLA | Community Designer Department of Transportation & Infrastructure | Transportation

f: 2025-04-24_09-28-03 - RE_ Alameda Lane Reduction - Lincoln to Franklin (2).pdf
s: RE: Alameda Lane Reduction - Lincoln to Franklin
to: Kashmann, Paul J. - CC Member District 6 Denver City Council <Paul.Kashmann@denvergov.org>
b:
Morning Councilman Kashmann-
My colleague and I just went and flyered the businesses last week, letting them know that construction should
begin in the summer and last approximately 4 months on a rolling basis. We will give them another heads up
once we know exact timing at least 2 weeks before. We will also let you all know this as well to include in your
communications. It will start at Lincoln and move its way to Franklin. We handed out the attached letter and are
also going to post this information to the website in the coming days. I am happy to answer any other questions
you may have.
Thank you! Molly
Molly Lanphier, MPH | Community Designer Department of Transportation & Infrastructure | Transportation
Implementation Division 720.913.4549 Phone | 720.619.1018 Cell

b:
Hi Brittany-
One option inside the Enterprise Map Viewer Form, is to leave all the “Search Criteria“ fields blank and then just
click the “Search” button. From there, a list of projects all projects you currently have access to will appear
inside the “Search Results” table. Select all the projects from the Search Results list- and then click the
“Generate Map View” button, like circled.
From there, you can enter the specific location in the Address Search Bar (like highlighted), to determine if
there are other projects in that location.
Thanks,
Emily Louie | Program Manager City & County of Denver Department of Transportation & Infrastructure |

<Bret.Guderian@denvergov.org>; Reuter, Patience - DOTI Program Management Director

<Richard.Keyes@denvergov.org>; Louie, Emily - DOTI CA1714 Program Manager
<Emily.Louie@denvergov.org> Subject: RE: GIS Project Mapping
Patience/MW Team,
Do you or your team have a step by step guide for someone who wanted to use Masterworks to display a map
of every project on a specific corridor or area? For example, I was asked yesterday to produce a map of all the
projects on Alameda Ave. I tried to use Masterworks but couldn’t figure out how to look up projects by the map.
(It seems like you have to already know the project names and then select to display them… what if I don’t
know the names?)
I ended up using the One Build map tool… which I think gets its project data from Masterworks. Is this the best
way?
Thanks, Brittany

Hi all –
Thank you for the discussion today. I think we made a lot of progress in 30 minutes. Here are my notes from our
meeting:
Two options were considered for restaffing active CMGC projects to help improve efficiency of the team. The
following option will be moved forward:
Morrison
* Dallas H. (PM support - invoicing) * Scott Reed (PM, staff aug) Buchtel
* Eric P. (PM) 5280
* Sam B. (PM) * Greg C. (PM Support) Washington (Sept construction NTP)
* Mark G. – He will lead through Construction NTP, with plan to replace him in fall with rep from Construction
team
Projects that may be held due to staffing issues (lack of construction PM), changes above will help:
* Federal Medians (Dallas) * Federal/Amherst * VZ Ped 2020/21 & Steele/Alameda (Kristine) * SMS Colfax
(remove) * Mill 10 * Others? Please let me know. We need to compile a list.
Communication of staffing changes:
* Brittany to communicate to bond team and DOTI leadership * Supervisors to communicate to staff who are
being moved (Ken to talk with Dallas, Amy to talk with Sam, Brittany to talk with Mark, Katie to talk with Eric P,
Brittany to talk with Greg)
Thanks, Brittany

s: FW: Questions on E. Alameda project....
to: "Holloway; Tykus R. - DOTI Deputy Manager" <Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org>; "Hillhouse; Jennifer L. -
b:
Questions being asked… NK
From: Mark Samuelson <mark@samuelsonassoc.com> Sent: Monday, May 12, 2025 4:02 PM To: Kuhn,
Nancy - DOTI Marketing and Communications Director <Nancy.Kuhn@denvergov.org> Subject: [EXTERNAL]
Questions on E. Alameda project....
Hi Nancy! Couple of questions on that, after having gone through the materials...
1. I assume basic goal remains to improve safety and reduce serious accidents? I read about the HIN accident
corridor focus, and assume that’s still primary... 2. Went thru the video of the zoom meeting on this in 2021. Am
I correct that the plan to close Alameda down to just two thru lanes at Downing and Corona is a new element to
the plan? I thought I recall in the meeting that those intersections would remain as before. 3. Are you holding
another public meeting before the start of construction on that? Are area residents aware of the final plan for the
entire stretch, including at Downing and Corona? 4. When do you plan for construction to begin? 5. Looks like
that 5-foot buffer on both the north and south sides of Alameda, east of Pearl to Humboldt. Are those to become
bike lanes? Would they have flex posts or other vertical structures? 6. Are you planning speed cameras as part
of the project? 7. I read in your details that there had been a previous attempt to reduce the lanes a decade ago
that had caused backups, and that the new plan is based on traffic counts that have been revised downward?
When were those done?
Thanks, Nancy; feel free to call if I can clarify....
Mark Samuelson The Denver Gazette 303-641-0196
mark@marksamuelson.com<mailto:mark@marksamuelson.com> DenverGazette.com

Here is the information on the outreach that was completed as well as some additional touchpoints Molly and I
had with the two Council Districts:
Project Schedule
* Data Collection: October-December 2019 * Analysis/ Alternatives Evaluation: January-March 2020 * Public
Outreach: May 2020 * Design Procurement – August 2021 * Design: Fall 2023 * CM Kashmann Briefing:
11/21/23 * CW Alvidrez Debrief: 3/8/24 * Public Outreach: 3/20/24 * CW Alvidrez Briefing: 4/17/25 – reviewed
before and after conditions * Constant Contact Notification on 2/4/25 – attached * Flyered corridor on April 14,
2025 – attached
If you need copies of the briefings we held with council, please let us know. I am also attaching the Before and
After Design comparison that CW Alvidrez asked to see to better understand the proposed changes.
Thanks, Greg

b:
Here’s what I sent, for future reference, after various tweaks and fixing a few typos. n I won’t be surprised to
see a few follow up questions. Thanks all. NK
From: Kuhn, Nancy - DOTI Marketing and Communications Director Sent: Wednesday, May 14, 2025 3:09 PM
To: 'mark@marksamuelson.com' <mark@marksamuelson.com> Subject: RE: Questions on E. Alameda
project....
Hi Mark,
Starting with a little overview:
Yes, the basic goals are to improve safety and reduce crashes. Alameda Avenue is on our high injury network –
the streets where most fatal and serious injury crashes are occurring in the city. We began hearing concerns
about safety on Alameda Ave between Lincoln and Franklin streets from the community a few years back. We
did an analysis and found issues of speeding and vehicle rear-end and turning collisions due to a lack of left
hand turn lanes. The community was also requesting pedestrian crossing improvements to help people get to
the nearby school and businesses along Alameda more easily and safely, given all the vehicle activity.
With that, we developed a design that would work to slow vehicle speeds, reduce incidents of rear end and left
turn collisions and minimize conflict for all users traveling along the corridor. The design does not change the
lane configuration along Alameda from Lincoln to Logan. The design provides drivers one vehicle travel lane in
each direction on Alameda Avenue between Pennsylvania and Franklin, adding a center turning lane in many
sections for safer turning movements - a place for drivers to be while they wait to make a turn, preventing
backups in the through-travel lane and the unsafe maneuvers drivers sometimes take to get around cars
waiting to turn.
We went into the community last year to update residents on the proposed design. Shortly thereafter, in June,
we launched our SPEED initiative<https://denvergov.org/Government/Agencies-Departments-Offices/Agencies
-Departments-Offices-Directory/Department-of-Transportation-and-Infrastructure/News-Outreach/News/Reduc
e-Traffic-Fatalities>, which is deploying other targeted actions to reduce speeds on Alameda Avenue from city
limit to city limit. The SPEED measures we have completed since June include:
* Saturating the corridor with speed limit signage, increasing sign density to approximately every ¼ mile to raise
awareness of the speed limit. * Placing additional electronic feedback signs along the corridor to communicate
driver speeds. * Programming traffic signals to slow vehicles down to the posted speed limits. * Shortening
signal cycle lengths in the overnight hours, reducing the amount of time that signals rest in green, discouraging
speeding when vehicle volumes are low. * Increasing the number of intersections on Federal and Alameda with
leading pedestrian intervals, or LPIs. LPIs give pedestrians a head start crossing the street and an opportunity
to establish themselves in an intersection for greater visibility before cars get the green. * Worked with Xcel to
increase visibility on the corridors by addressing any damaged/dark street lights * Completed a Slow Down for
the Love of Safety billboard campaign, telling people to Slow Down. Picture attached.
Up next: The SPEED initiative also calls for deploying fixed, automated speed enforcement cameras on
Alameda Ave and Federal Blvd. We just learned that in late spring/early summer, we will have a chance to try
out the cameras – not for speed enforcement (people won’t be getting tickets) - but to see how they work in
terms of gauging speeds as well as the number of people who are speeding on the corridor and how our
interventions have worked so far.
The testing of these cameras may push out our implementation of the lane repurposing project a bit, also
affording us some time to address any new or lingering community concerns about the lane repurposing plan.
Responses to your questions are below.
I assume basic goal remains to improve safety and reduce serious accidents? I read about the HIN accident
corridor focus, and assume that’s still primary... Correct
Went thru the video of the zoom meeting on this in 2021. Am I correct that the plan to close Alameda down to
just two thru lanes at Downing and MARION is a new element to the plan? I thought I recall in the meeting that
those intersections would remain as before. There are currently two travel lanes on Alameda in each direction
at the intersections of Downing and Marion and no left turns are permitted from Alameda onto Downing or
Marion. Here, it will go to one lane in each direction, consistent with what’s happening on either side. No left
turns will be permitted, as is the case right now. The center or middle travel lanes of Alameda at these
intersections will be re-striped to offer a safer pedestrian crossing. Pedestrians will cross two vehicle travel
lanes on Alameda instead of four, and the striping plan will provide a mid-block pedestrian refuge. Here’s a
visual of that:
Are you holding another public meeting before the start of construction on that? Are area residents aware of the
final plan for the entire stretch, including at Downing and MARION? Below is a look at some of the outreach
we’ve done so far and recently. We typically engage the community through traditional media and social media
as we get closer to construction and will review what other methods might be needed or helpful as we get
closer. My understanding is that businesses along this stretch have been supportive.
Data Collection: October-December 2019 Analysis/ Alternatives Evaluation: January-March 2020 Public
Outreach: May 2020 Design Procurement – August 2021 Design: Fall 2023 Public Outreach: 3/20/24 Constant
Contact Notification on 2/4/25 – attached Flyered corridor on April 14, 2025 – attached
When do you plan for construction to begin? No confirmed date. Per note above, we want to test out the fixed
camera technology on Alameda and Federal; that may adjust the anticipated schedule for the Alameda lane
repurposing work, which was planned for mid-year/summer 2025.
Looks like that 5-foot buffer on both the north and south sides of Alameda, east of Pearl to Humboldt. Are those
to become bike lanes? Would they have flex posts or other vertical structures? No plans to install a bike lane.
The buffer supports narrowing the roadway to one lane in each direction.
Are you planning speed cameras as part of the project? Speed cameras are a separate effort, but yes, our goal
is to deploy fixed, automatic speed camera technology, starting on Federal and Alameda corridors. We will also
be doing community outreach and engagement with the public to discuss the program, how it would work, the
technology and our approach to safety as part of the program development.
I read in your details that there had been a previous attempt to reduce the lanes a decade ago that had caused
backups, and that the new plan is based on traffic counts that have been revised downward? When were those
done? DOTI installed a lane reduction on Alameda back in the 2008-2010 timeframe in the eastbound direction
at Downing, which resulted in a stacking of vehicles on the eastbound approach to Downing Street. During that
time, the Alameda average daily traffic was over 20,000. Average daily traffic on that stretch is now much lower,
so Alameda is a better candidate now for a lane reduction.
Thanks, Nancy; feel free to call if I can clarify....
Mark Samuelson The Denver Gazette 303-641-0196
mark@marksamuelson.com<mailto:mark@marksamuelson.com> DenverGazette.com

f: 2025-05-23_10-32-05 - Re Alameda Lane Reduction Project - Concerned Stakeholders M.pdf
s: RE: Alameda Lane Reduction Project - Concerned Stakeholders Meeting
cc: Holloway, Tykus R. - DOTI Deputy Manager <Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org>; Price, Brittany C. - DOTI
b:
Hi Jason-
Sorry for the delay. Brittany asked me to coordinate some times that may work for you all. I know school is
almost out and I would assume this will affect traffic patterns. It looks like Steele Elementary starts at 8:05 am
and ends at 3:05pm. Given that, could you make either May 30th at 2:30pm or June 2 at 7:30 am work? If not,
we can go further out. I just know DPS’ last day is June 3rd.
Thank you! Molly

From: Jason Gallardo <jason@mgpublicaffairs.com<mailto:jason@mgpublicaffairs.com>> Sent: Wednesday,
May 14, 2025 9:44 AM To: Price, Brittany C. - DOTI CE2781 Engineer-Architect Manager
<Brittany.Price@denvergov.org<mailto:Brittany.Price@denvergov.org>> Cc: Holloway, Tykus R. - DOTI
Deputy Manager <Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org<mailto:Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org>> Subject:
[EXTERNAL] Re: Alameda Lane Reduction Project - Concerned Stakeholders Meeting
Hi Brittany,
Thanks for reaching out. Yes, I agree that it would be helpful to get everyone together—including the concerned
neighbors—for a productive discussion about the Alameda Lane Reduction project.
We’d prefer an in-person meeting that includes a walkthrough of the area during peak traffic hours so we can
see the impacts firsthand. This will give us all a better understanding of the issues being raised.
Some of the primary concerns we’ve heard include: (cid:127) Turn lane backups and overflow into through lanes,
causing significant traffic delays (cid:127) Increased neighborhood cut-through traffic, raising safety concerns due to
speeding and reckless driving (cid:127) Higher vehicle volumes on Virginia, which isn’t designed to support that level of
traffic, possibly requiring more DOTI involvement
Please let me know your availability and any proposed dates/times that work on your end, and we can
coordinate from there.
Best,
Jason A. Gallardo J.D. Partner | MG Public Affairs www.mgpublicaffairs.com<https:/www.mgpublicaffairs.com>

cc: "Price; Brittany C. - DOTI CE2781 Engineer-Architect Manager" <Brittany.Price@denvergov.org>;
b:
Dallas,
I typically work with Brittany Price on Milestone updates for Bond Projects each month. With Brittany out of
office, I am reaching out directly to PMs for the Bond Milestone Updates this month. If you could respond to this
email with Milestone Updates by EOD Wednesday June 4th, that would be appreciated!
Federal Blvd Ped Improvements: Final Complete: 12/23/2026 (Bond) or 4/1/2025 (Masterworks)
NTSPI – Alameda Lane Reduction: Logan to Franklin: Construction Ad: 5/30/2025 - Do we have an actual or do
we need to re-baseline the Ad date? Construction NTP: 7/30/2025 Substantial Complete: 12/23/2025 Final
Complete: 3/30/2026
Please confirm if dates are still accurate or list new baseline dates in RED
Thank you!
Caleb Flack | Portfolio Coordinator/Program Controls Elevate Denver Bond Program | City and County of
Denver p: 303-882-5757 | caleb.flack@denvergov.org<mailto:melissa.martinez2@denvergov.org>
<mailto:melissa.martinez2@denvergov.org> [cid:image001.png@01DBCFC5.ABA202B0]

No, no timeline yet. I can let you know, though! I’d likely be doing a press release in advance of the
adjustments. Nancy

f: 2025-06-05_20-31-45 - _EXTERNAL_ Concerns about Alameda Lane Repurposing project.pdf
s: Concerns about Alameda Lane Repurposing project
b:
Dear Ms. Ford, I am writing to you as a resident of the neighborhood where DOTI’s “Alameda Lane
Repurposing” project is slated to take place this summer. (I live at E Alameda Ave & Race St.) As a daily driver
and pedestrian in this area, I have serious concerns about the project, which I believe are shared by many other
residents – if they’re even aware. I’ve summarized three of my concerns below. I would appreciate any
consideration you can give to this. First, neighborhood access to E Alameda Ave will be significantly
compromised. Neighbors like me relying on Alameda to exit the area will face longer delays, especially during
peak times. I live a couple blocks off Alameda and already struggle to turn onto Alameda from my residential
street. It’s not uncommon for me to wait 8-10 minutes to turn left onto Alameda when I’m taking my kids to
school in the morning. (This is truly not an exaggeration! I have timed it with my phone’s stopwatch many times
to plan for our school route.) For example, when traffic that is currently flowing in two lanes is combined into a
single lane and then stopped at the lights near Downing St, more residential streets will be effectively blocked
during the light cycle by the increased backup, which will slow residents’ efforts to turn onto Alameda and move
on to other areas. I’m also very concerned about the areas where DOTI plans to block left turns from Alameda
onto residential streets with medians or other structures; I worry that an unintended consequence of that plan is
that residents on those cross streets will no longer be able to turn left from their street onto Alameda. The
impact of these changes is exacerbated by the location of Wash Park to the south and the Denver Country Club
to the north of E Alameda Ave along the stretch that will be modified; these large landmarks limit the ways we
can access our residential streets and areas. Now that DOTI has “repurposed” a number of lanes in other
residential areas, people in those communities report that frustrated drivers divert to their quiet residential
streets. Often these drivers who divert drive aggressively to vent their feelings about the change in the traffic
patterns. There is so much pedestrian traffic throughout these residential cross-streets. It would be an error to
divert traffic there! This leads to my next point: Unintended safety risks will emerge after the project is
implemented. I have carefully reviewed all the publicly available information about this project. I was shocked to
see that DOTI’s plans anticipate that traffic from Alameda will divert to Virginia Avenue as a result of the
changes and DOTI does not see this as an issue. Virginia Avenue is a quiet, residential street that is adjacent to
one of our city’s busiest parks, Wash Park. This street lacks the infrastructure needed to safely handle
increased traffic volumes. I am extremely concerned about new safety issues for pedestrians, cyclists and
park-goers if there is more traffic on Virginia Ave. I’d anticipate that this will require future intervention by DOTI.
Lastly, DOTI has not conducted enough community engagement around this project. I have mentioned this
project to dozens of residents in this area and not a single person is aware of the plans. (Once aware, they
share my surprise at what's proposed!) DOTI conducted just one community meeting about this project in early
2024 and then provided no new information for over a year. I really do not believe this will be well received. In
the scope of DOTI’s projects, I imagine this is not a major one. But to residents of these neighborhoods and
regular commuters along this stretch of Alameda, this will be a massive change to their daily route that likely
adds time and frustration. I encourage DOTI to further engage the community about this project and determine
if the benefits truly outweigh the negatives. Sincerely, Jill Anschutz
Jill.hamilton@mac.com<mailto:Jill.hamilton@mac.com> 502.938.2947

f: 2025-06-06_08-58-33 - RE_ _EXTERNAL_ Concerns about Alameda Lane Repurposing proje.pdf
s: RE: Concerns about Alameda Lane Repurposing project
cc: "Holloway; Tykus R. - DOTI Deputy Manager" <Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org>; "Cieciek; Gregory M. -
b:
Jill,
Thank you very much for your note and thoughtful insight about the proposed project. I’ve included our team
members who are working on this plan in this email response and am aware that perhaps the team is working
to organize a meeting with the key stakeholders and community members to discuss the project in more detail.
We would like to include you in that discussion.
We appreciate that when we embark on projects like this to seek to address some of the many safety issues
along the corridor (regular crashes, many of which attach to the left hand turn movements of folks, the queuing
in the lanes and folks driving around), as well as safety issues with crossings for pedestrians and other, that
how we approach the engineering solutions needs to be carefully reviewed.
We look forward to continuing the discussion with you and the community.
Amy
Amy Ford | Executive Director City & County of Denver Department of Transportation & Infrastructure Phone:
(303) 514-4913 amy.ford@denvergov.org<mailto:amy.ford@denvergov.org>

Jill.hamilton@mac.com<mailto:Jill.hamilton@mac.com> 502.938.2947

Dear Ms Ford,
I am part of a community of neighbors who are very concerned with DOTI's upcoming planned "Alameda Lane
Repurposing" project. I live on Marion Pkwy, just north of Alameda (between Cedar and Alameda) and use this
and the surrounding streets every single day to get to and from my home. I also have a busy small business in
Wash Park (at Kentucky and Pearl) that I have operated for 11 years and myself and clients rely heavily on
Alameda and the streets surrounding to get to and from my business.
Alemeda is one of the major roads Wash Park residents and visitors rely on to get to their homes, the park,
businesses and schools in the community. To reduce the capacity of this street to two lanes will significantly
impact traffic in the neighborhood. Not only will Alameda itself get overcrowded, but I am specifically concerned
about the impact and crowding that will ultimately spill over onto Downing (which is already extremely
backed-up in Wash Park during rush hour), access to University and the ability to get to Cherry Creek North
(the only other major access from Wash Park is Speer, which itself is overcrowded) and Marion Pkwy (which is
a major host to bicyclists, kids at Steele Elementary School and access to the extremely busy Washington
Park). The last thing this busy neighborhood needs is less room on this major thoroughfare and more traffic. It
will force people to divert into the neighborhood streets, endangering pedestrians with speeding cars trying to
"cut-through" to get to their destinations faster. I already am a witness first-hand the danger that cars traveling
on these "side streets" pose to kids, bikers, scooters and runners that are headed in and out of the park,
specifically on Marion Pkwy and Virgina at the north entrance into the park itself. I also personally find it nearly
impossible to currently turn west out of my alley and onto Alameda in the mornings due to an already
overcrowded street with the current configuration. If and when it goes to two lanes, I and my neighbors on the
north side of Alameda might truly not be able to access Alameda out of our alley at all during rush hour due to a
complete back-up of cars.
As a resident and business owner in Wash Park, I send this email with deep concern about the impact of this
project. Driving Alameda daily, I know the impact fewer lanes will cause. Wash Park residents use Alemeda to
access their home streets, and that alone (someone waiting to turn off onto their street) will cause a HUGE
backup consistently. Plus, I do wonder how the myriad of businesses on Alameda feel about the fewer lanes,
thus, less people and less access of clients to their businesses. I hope that DOTI will consider these negative
impacts and choose to pause this project to adequately assess its reasoning and to also reach out and engage
more input from the community it will impact first-hand.
Thanks for taking the time to read and consider,
Kimberly Kolesar kim@psdenver.com<mailto:kim@psdenver.com> 925-200-6260
--
Kimberly Kolesar OWNER | THE PILATES STUDIO Wash Park-Highlands Nationally Certified Pilates Teacher,
NCPT 303-733-3833 kim@psdenver.com<mailto:amy@psdenver.com>

on these "side streets" pose to kids, bikers, scooters and runners that are headed in and out of the park,
specifically on Marion Pkwy and Virgina at the north entrance into the park itself. I also personally find it nearly
impossible to currently turn west out of my alley and onto Alameda in the mornings due to an already
overcrowded street with the current configuration. If and when it goes to two lanes, I and my neighbors on the
north side of Alameda might truly not be able to access Alameda out of our alley at all during rush hour due to a
complete back-up of cars.
As a resident and business owner in Wash Park, I send this email with deep concern about the impact of this
project. Driving Alameda daily, I know the impact fewer lanes will cause. Wash Park residents use Alemeda to
access their home streets, and that alone (someone waiting to turn off onto their street) will cause a HUGE
backup consistently. Plus, I do wonder how the myriad of businesses on Alameda feel about the fewer lanes,
thus, less people and less access of clients to their businesses. I hope that DOTI will consider these negative
impacts and choose to pause this project to adequately assess its reasoning and to also reach out and engage
more input from the community it will impact first-hand.
Thanks for taking the time to read and consider,
Kimberly Kolesar kim@psdenver.com<mailto:kim@psdenver.com> 925-200-6260

f: 2025-06-09_08-42-57 - Fw_ _EXTERNAL_ Concerns about DOTI's _Alameda Lane Repurposi.pdf
s: Fw: Concerns about DOTI's "Alameda Lane Repurposing"
to: Cieciek, Gregory M. - DOTI CE2294 Project Manager II Engineering <Gregory.Cieciek@denvergov.org>;
b:
Get Outlook for iOS<https://aka.ms/o0ukef> ________________________________ From: Kimberly Kolesar
<kim@psdenver.com> Sent: Sunday, June 8, 2025 10:19:12 PM To: Ford, Amy - DOTI HE3258 Manager
Department of Transportation and Infrastructure <Amy.Ford@denvergov.org> Subject: [EXTERNAL] Concerns
about DOTI's "Alameda Lane Repurposing" project
Dear Ms Ford,
I am part of a community of neighbors who are very concerned with DOTI's upcoming planned "Alameda Lane
Repurposing" project. I live on Marion Pkwy, just north of Alameda (between Cedar and Alameda) and use this
and the surrounding streets every single day to get to and from my home. I also have a busy small business in
Wash Park (at Kentucky and Pearl) that I have operated for 11 years and myself and clients rely heavily on
Alameda and the streets surrounding to get to and from my business.
Alemeda is one of the major roads Wash Park residents and visitors rely on to get to their homes, the park,
businesses and schools in the community. To reduce the capacity of this street to two lanes will significantly
impact traffic in the neighborhood. Not only will Alameda itself get overcrowded, but I am specifically concerned
about the impact and crowding that will ultimately spill over onto Downing (which is already extremely
backed-up in Wash Park during rush hour), access to University and the ability to get to Cherry Creek North
(the only other major access from Wash Park is Speer, which itself is overcrowded) and Marion Pkwy (which is
a major host to bicyclists, kids at Steele Elementary School and access to the extremely busy Washington
Park). The last thing this busy neighborhood needs is less room on this major thoroughfare and more traffic. It
will force people to divert into the neighborhood streets, endangering pedestrians with speeding cars trying to
"cut-through" to get to their destinations faster. I already am a witness first-hand the danger that cars traveling
on these "side streets" pose to kids, bikers, scooters and runners that are headed in and out of the park,
specifically on Marion Pkwy and Virgina at the north entrance into the park itself. I also personally find it nearly
impossible to currently turn west out of my alley and onto Alameda in the mornings due to an already
overcrowded street with the current configuration. If and when it goes to two lanes, I and my neighbors on the
north side of Alameda might truly not be able to access Alameda out of our alley at all during rush hour due to a
complete back-up of cars.
As a resident and business owner in Wash Park, I send this email with deep concern about the impact of this
project. Driving Alameda daily, I know the impact fewer lanes will cause. Wash Park residents use Alemeda to
access their home streets, and that alone (someone waiting to turn off onto their street) will cause a HUGE
backup consistently. Plus, I do wonder how the myriad of businesses on Alameda feel about the fewer lanes,
thus, less people and less access of clients to their businesses. I hope that DOTI will consider these negative
impacts and choose to pause this project to adequately assess its reasoning and to also reach out and engage
more input from the community it will impact first-hand.
Thanks for taking the time to read and consider,
Kimberly Kolesar kim@psdenver.com<mailto:kim@psdenver.com> 925-200-6260
--
Kimberly Kolesar OWNER | THE PILATES STUDIO Wash Park-Highlands Nationally Certified Pilates Teacher,

f: 2025-06-11_22-19-29 - _EXTERNAL_ Concerns about Proposed Changes to East Alameda A.pdf
s: Concerns about Proposed Changes to East Alameda Ave
b:
Ms. Ford, Mr. Holloway and Mr. Williams, I am a resident and homeowner in Central / East Denver (80220) and
am reaching out to express concerns about proposed changes to East Alameda Avenue, which I read about in
the Denver Gazette a few weeks ago: https://denvergazette.com/news/alameda-road-diet-denver-traffic-lanes/a
rticle_61ff249d-fe60-44e5-b188-20e6d29eee5c.html<https://denvergazette.com/news/alameda-road-diet-denv
er-traffic-lanes/article_61ff249d-fe60-44e5-b188-20e6d29eee5c.html>
I count on East Alameda as a primary east / west thoroughfare and am concerned that the proposed changes
will reduce capacity and result in increased congestion, and related safety concerns. I worry, particularly, for
local residents who will see increased traffic on side roads that are not built to handle that volume.
I believe you all have good intentions but worry that there will be unintended consequences from these
changes. I urge you to slow down this process, do further engagement with impacted Denverites and carefully
consider the risks of the proposed changes.
Thank you for your consideration. Cody Belzley Denver, CO 80220 303 913 1453

f: 2025-06-12_09-58-27 - _EXTERNAL_ Heads Up DSST Team___more traffic headed your way.pdf
s: Heads Up DSST Team...more traffic headed your way!
b:
Dear DOTI, I recently read an article in the Denver Gazette about the East Alameda Lane Repurposing project
that left me wondering how cutting the number of lanes on Alameda could possibly be a good idea. https://denv
ergazette.com/news/alameda-road-diet-denver-traffic-lanes/article_61ff249d-fe60-44e5-b188-20e6d29eee5c.ht
ml<https://denvergazette.com/news/alameda-road-diet-denver-traffic-lanes/article_61ff249d-fe60-44e5-b188-2
0e6d29eee5c.html>
As a DPS parent who takes my kids to school in that area every morning (and thus drives Alameda often) I am
concerned about the ramifications to what is already a busy street. Our school (DSST Cedar) already struggles
with traffic congestion at both drop off and pick up. And getting in and out of the neighborhood already requires
that I build in quite a bit of extra travel time. There are actually mornings where I sit on Alameda waiting for the
light at University to cycle through for upwards of 10-12 minutes. I can't see how your plan to reduce lanes will
improve traffic. I'm also concerned that this will make commuters use the side streets – which I assume is less
efficient, less safe and not a great solution for the people who live/work/go to school in the neighborhood.
I've added the leadership at DSST to make sure they are aware of your plans. Traffic is already an issue for our
little community...and I can't begin to see how this will help.
I hope you'll reconsider.
Amy Guttmann
x:
Heads Up DSST Team...more traffic headed your way! Amy Guttmann amymguttmann@gmail.com tykus.holloway@denvergov.org; amy.ford@denvergov.org; nicholas.williams@denvergov.org; Bailey Dear DOTI, I recently read an article in the Denver Gazette about the East Alameda Lane Repurposing project
that left me wondering how cutting the number of lanes on Alameda could possibly be a good idea. https://denv
ergazette.com/news/alameda-road-diet-denver-traffic-lanes/article_61ff249d-fe60-44e5-b188-20e6d29eee5c.ht
ml<https://denvergazette.com/news/alameda-road-diet-denver-traffic-lanes/article_61ff249d-fe60-44e5-b188-2
0e6d29eee5c.html>
As a DPS parent who takes my kids to school in that area every morning (and thus drives Alameda often) I am
concerned about the ramifications to what is already a busy street. Our school (DSST Cedar) already struggles
with traffic congestion at both drop off and pick up. And getting in and out of the neighborhood already requires
that I build in quite a bit of extra travel time. There are actually mornings where I sit on Alameda waiting for the
light at University to cycle through for upwards of 10-12 minutes. I can't see how your plan to reduce lanes will
improve traffic. I'm also concerned that this will make commuters use the side streets – which I assume is less
efficient, less safe and not a great solution for the people who live/work/go to school in the neighborhood.
I've added the leadership at DSST to make sure they are aware of your plans. Traffic is already an issue for our
little community...and I can't begin to see how this will help.
I hope you'll reconsider.
Amy Guttmann

f: 2025-06-13_16-35-00 - _EXTERNAL_ Concerns about proposed changes to Alameda.pdf
s: Concerns about proposed changes to Alameda
b:
Dear Ms. Ford,
I'm writing to express a few concerns about changes to Alameda Avenue that recently have been proposed.
My primary concern is with the idea of repurposing traffic lines along Alameda, turning four lanes of traffic into
just two, as referenced in a recent article from the Denver Gazette<https://denvergazette.com/news/alameda-r
oad-diet-denver-traffic-lanes/article_61ff249d-fe60-44e5-b188-20e6d29eee5c.html>.
Turning travel lanes into turn lanes will not reduce the need for vehicle traffic through this corridor, but it will
reduce overall capacity. The result? Increased traffic congestion. This will create backups into the proposed
new turn lanes and remaining travel lanes, especially during high-traffic times of day.
I'm also concerned about ripple effects. While drivers may avoid Alameda, it is likely that they will continue to
travel by vehicle through the neighborhood, but use residential streets instead, especially Virginia Avenue,
which is not equipped to handle increased traffic. New safety issues will be created by the attempt to address
safety issues along Alameda.
Furthermore, I have not seen compelling safety data to justify this redesign. Instead, it seems like an expensive
project based on theoretical benefits rather than real-world conditions or a strong desire from the community.
I'm troubled by the lack of data to justify this change and by the lack of community engagement to create a
thoughtful approach to addressing legitimate community concerns.
I strongly urge DOTI to slow down this project and engage the neighborhood in a broader, more productive
dialogue to address community level traffic and safety concerns.
Sincerely,
Illana Poley

cc: "Piper; Samuel - DOTI CE3124 City Planning Manager" <Samuel.Piper@denvergov.org>; "Vigil; Rachael -
b:
Hi Community Design Team,
I wanted to update you all that we’re still on track to get the package of pedestrian crossing projects referenced
below out to bid this month and starting construction this fall. To that end, Kayla and I are working over the next
this month to develop informational flyers for the high/medium risk projects and the Steele/Alameda projects.
Can you prioritize notifying Council about all of the projects (all risk levels) by mid-July as well? Please let us
know if you hear any issues or concerns from them. Starting in mid-July, we’ll target notifying RNOs/any
relevant stakeholders, flyer the corridors of the high/medium risk projects, and meet with the two
Steele/Alameda stakeholders.
Please mark when you’ve notified council in column U of this spreadsheet<https://denvercity-my.sharepoint.co
m/:x:/r/personal/rachael_vigil_denvergov_org/Documents/Documents/Rachael%27s%20Documents/Projects/M
aster%20Ped%20Document.xlsx?d=w209d0e62bbcf4793bcd6d69c175f225a&csf=1&web=1&e=G006RG>. As
a reminder, all the projects in column F marked as “Pre Construction” are those that are in this package. Greg,
I’ve noted that you notified Hinds about Lincoln/11th.
Thanks all,
Paige

See below for our revised outreach strategy for the upcoming pedestrian crossing program construction
package. As a reminder, here is the overall schedule we’re looking at:
* Construction Procurement – beginning of summer, late May * Construction – Fall
Besides Council outreach, we are holding all the other outreach strategies until we start construction
procurement since we’ll have a better timeline at that point. To confirm, is that the approach we want to take for
the Steele/Alameda projects? These require a more complex level of outreach and I had written “as soon as

Paul

but I do know that Jill Anschutz is one and may be in attendance.

attendance.
Thank you- Molly

to: Good, Karen A. - DOTI CE3476 Project Manager Engineering Supervisor <Karen.Good@denvergov.org>;
cc: Weis, Gavin - DOTI CE2291 Project Manager I Engineering <Gavin.Weis@denvergov.org>
b:
Hi Karen,
Yes. I have a few updates that were missed. * In our Safe and Slow Streets presentation, we indicated that 29th
Ave and Williams St were “Complete” as I understood this to be substantially complete. The website now says,
“Mostly complete with final items to be installed by end of summer 2025.” Any issues with just saying
“Complete?” Copying @Weis, Gavin<mailto:Gavin.Weis@denvergov.org> for insight. * The 2024 bike paving
project details all reference “DOTI is moving forward with a design…” Since these were already built, I think we
need to change the tense to be “DOTI constructed a bikeway on X street that included the following
elements:…” * 7th Ave: Mariposa Street to Delaware Street Project Status should match the timeline for the
other NTMP projects (“Installation planned for fall 2025”). And it needs a “Project Description: This
neighborhood bikeway will include pedestrian safety improvements such as curb extensions to shorten crossing
distances as well as traffic calming elements that slow down vehicle speeds such as speed cushions.” * W
Kentucky Ave needs a “Project Description: This project includes adding bikeway protection elements along the
Kentucky bike lane to prevent vehicle encroachment.” * W Ohio Ave needs a “Project Description: This project
includes adding a counterflow buffered bike lane to safely facilitate bike travel in the WB direction around
Huston Lake Park. It will also include pedestrian safety improvements such as curb extensions at intersections
and additional pedestrian crossing markings.” * We should add the Knox Ct Neighborhood Bikeway upgrades
from Kentucky to Alameda which are being carried forward through the Fall 2025 NTMP project. Details: *
Neighborhood: Westwood * Project Status: Installation planned for fall 2025 * Project Description: This
neighborhood bikeway upgrade will include pedestrian safety improvements such as pedestrian striping and
signage, traffic calming that will slow down vehicle speeds, and wayfinding signage to bring the neighborhood
bikeway up the DOTI’s latest bikeway standards. * Program: Neighborhood Transportation Management
Program (NTMP) * Market Street Status should show as Complete * GVR project status needs to be updated.
@Pogue, Eric<mailto:Eric.Pogue@denvergov.org> – Can you indicate when construction is slated to be
substantially complete? Also, this was not an NTMP project. Program would be Bond I believe? * Uvalda is all
complete I believe. @Vigil, Rachael<mailto:Rachael.Vigil@denvergov.org> can you confirm?
Thanks all!
Brett Boncore, PE| Engineering Supervisor City & County of Denver Department of Transportation &
Infrastructure | Transportation Design Phone: 720.865.3214
brett.boncore@denvergov.org<mailto:brett.boncore@denvergov.org>

at an intersection) I didn’t want to advertise them as complete. We can change that if you want but we may get
questions as to why the RRFBs aren’t functioning if they are done.
Thanks,

cc: "Holloway; Tykus R. - DOTI Deputy Manager" <Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org>; "Lanphier; Molly - DOTI
b:
Hi Ms. Kolesar,
I’m reaching out on behalf of Tykus Holloway, as one of the Community Designers for the council districts
where the Alameda Lane Repurposing project is located.
Thank you very much for your note and the thoughtful insight you've shared regarding the proposed Alameda
Lane Repurposing project. We sincerely appreciate you taking the time to share your perspective both as a
resident and a local business owner in the Wash Park neighborhood.
We understand that any changes to a corridor like Alameda may change travel patterns—especially to those
who rely on it daily. Your concerns about traffic circulation, neighborhood safety, business access, and overall
connectivity are important to us as we continue evaluating how best to improve safety and mobility in this area.
As part of our process, we are currently reviewing all the comments we've received and will provide an update
in the coming months. We recognize that projects like this require careful consideration of the trade-offs and
potential unintended impacts, and we are committed to ensuring that those are part of our ongoing assessment.
Again, thank you for sharing your perspective. We look forward to continuing the discussion and working
together toward a solution that prioritizes safety while supporting the daily needs of residents, businesses, and
all who use the corridor.
Regards, Greg

From: Kimberly Kolesar <kim@psdenver.com> Sent: Sunday, June 8, 2025 10:17 PM To: Holloway, Tykus R. -
DOTI Deputy Manager <Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org> Subject: [EXTERNAL] Concerns about DOTI's
"Alameda Lane Repurposing" project. I live on Marion Pkwy, just north of Alameda (between Cedar
and Alameda) and use this
Dear Mr. Holloway,
I am part of a community of neighbors who are very concerned with DOTI's upcoming planned "Alameda Lane
Repurposing" project. I live on Marion Pkwy, just north of Alameda (between Cedar and Alameda) and use this
and the surrounding streets every single day to get to and from my home. I also have a busy small business in
Wash Park (at Kentucky and Pearl) that I have operated for 11 years and myself and clients rely heavily on
Alameda and the streets surrounding to get to and from my business.
Alemeda is one of the major roads Wash Park residents and visitors rely on to get to their homes, the park,
businesses and schools in the community. To reduce the capacity of this street to two lanes will significantly
impact traffic in the neighborhood. Not only will Alameda itself get overcrowded, but I am specifically concerned
about the impact and crowding that will ultimately spill over onto Downing (which is already extremely
backed-up in Wash Park during rush hour), access to University and the ability to get to Cherry Creek North
(the only other major access from Wash Park is Speer, which itself is overcrowded) and Marion Pkwy (which is
a major host to bicyclists, kids at Steele Elementary School and access to the extremely busy Washington
Park). The last thing this busy neighborhood needs is less room on this major thoroughfare and more traffic. It
will force people to divert into the neighborhood streets, endangering pedestrians with speeding cars trying to
"cut-through" to get to their destinations faster. I already am a witness first-hand the danger that cars traveling
on these "side streets" pose to kids, bikers, scooters and runners that are headed in and out of the park,
specifically on Marion Pkwy and Virgina at the north entrance into the park itself. I also personally find it nearly
impossible to currently turn west out of my alley and onto Alameda in the mornings due to an already
overcrowded street with the current configuration. If and when it goes to two lanes, I and my neighbors on the
north side of Alameda might truly not be able to access Alameda out of our alley at all during rush hour due to a
complete back-up of cars.
As a resident and business owner in Wash Park, I send this email with deep concern about the impact of this
project. Driving Alameda daily, I know the impact fewer lanes will cause. Wash Park residents use Alemeda to
access their home streets, and that alone (someone waiting to turn off onto their street) will cause a HUGE
backup consistently. Plus, I do wonder how the myriad of businesses on Alameda feel about the fewer lanes,
thus, less people and less access of clients to their businesses. I hope that DOTI will consider these negative
impacts and choose to pause this project to adequately assess its reasoning and to also reach out and engage
more input from the community it will impact first-hand.
Thanks for taking the time to read and consider,
Kimberly Kolesar kim@psdenver.com<mailto:kim@psdenver.com> 925-200-6260 -------

Concerns about Proposed Changes to East Alameda Ave
Ms. Ford, Mr. Holloway and Mr. Williams, I am a resident and homeowner in Central / East Denver (80220) and
am reaching out to express concerns about proposed changes to East Alameda Avenue, which I read about in
the Denver Gazette a few weeks ago: https://denvergazette.com/news/alameda-road-diet-denver-traffic-lanes/a
rticle_61ff249d-fe60-44e5-b188-20e6d29eee5c.html<https:/denvergazette.com/news/alameda-road-diet-denve
r-traffic-lanes/article_61ff249d-fe60-44e5-b188-20e6d29eee5c.html>
I count on East Alameda as a primary east / west thoroughfare and am concerned that the proposed changes
will reduce capacity and result in increased congestion, and related safety concerns. I worry, particularly, for
local residents who will see increased traffic on side roads that are not built to handle that volume.
I believe you all have good intentions but worry that there will be unintended consequences from these
changes. I urge you to slow down this process, do further engagement with impacted Denverites and carefully
consider the risks of the proposed changes.
Thank you for your consideration. Cody Belzley Denver, CO 80220 303 913 1453

webster.johnson@scienceandtech.org; elin.curry@scienceandtech.org; Casias, Angela C. - MO Executive
Officer <Angela.Casias@denvergov.org> Subject: [EXTERNAL] Heads Up DSST Team...more traffic headed
your way!
Dear DOTI, I recently read an article in the Denver Gazette about the East Alameda Lane Repurposing project
that left me wondering how cutting the number of lanes on Alameda could possibly be a good idea. https://denv
ergazette.com/news/alameda-road-diet-denver-traffic-lanes/article_61ff249d-fe60-44e5-b188-20e6d29eee5c.ht
ml<https:/denvergazette.com/news/alameda-road-diet-denver-traffic-lanes/article_61ff249d-fe60-44e5-b188-20
e6d29eee5c.html>
As a DPS parent who takes my kids to school in that area every morning (and thus drives Alameda often) I am
concerned about the ramifications to what is already a busy street. Our school (DSST Cedar) already struggles
with traffic congestion at both drop off and pick up. And getting in and out of the neighborhood already requires
that I build in quite a bit of extra travel time. There are actually mornings where I sit on Alameda waiting for the
light at University to cycle through for upwards of 10-12 minutes. I can't see how your plan to reduce lanes will
improve traffic. I'm also concerned that this will make commuters use the side streets – which I assume is less
efficient, less safe and not a great solution for the people who live/work/go to school in the neighborhood.
I've added the leadership at DSST to make sure they are aware of your plans. Traffic is already an issue for our
little community...and I can't begin to see how this will help.
I hope you'll reconsider.
Amy Guttmann

From: Illana Poley <illanapoley@gmail.com> Sent: Friday, June 13, 2025 4:36 PM To: Holloway, Tykus R. -
DOTI Deputy Manager <Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org> Subject: [EXTERNAL] Proposed changes to
Alameda
To Whom It May Concern:
I'm writing to express a few concerns about changes to Alameda Avenue that recently have been proposed.
My primary concern is with the idea of repurposing traffic lines along Alameda, turning four lanes of traffic into
just two, as referenced in a recent article from the Denver Gazette<https:/denvergazette.com/news/alameda-ro
ad-diet-denver-traffic-lanes/article_61ff249d-fe60-44e5-b188-20e6d29eee5c.html>.
Turning travel lanes into turn lanes will not reduce the need for vehicle traffic through this corridor, but it will
reduce overall capacity. The result? Increased traffic congestion. This will create backups into the proposed
new turn lanes and remaining travel lanes, especially during high-traffic times of day.
I'm also concerned about ripple effects. While drivers may avoid Alameda, it is likely that they will continue to
travel by vehicle through the neighborhood, but use residential streets instead, especially Virginia Avenue,
which is not equipped to handle increased traffic. New safety issues will be created by the attempt to address
safety issues along Alameda.
Furthermore, I have not seen compelling safety data to justify this redesign. Instead, it seems like an expensive
project based on theoretical benefits rather than real-world conditions or a strong desire from the community.
I'm troubled by the lack of data to justify this change and by the lack of community engagement to create a
thoughtful approach to addressing legitimate community concerns.
I strongly urge DOTI to slow down this project and engage the neighborhood in a broader, more productive
dialogue to address community level traffic and safety concerns.
Sincerely,
Illana Poley

crawfordgirl@yahoo.com<mailto:crawfordgirl@yahoo.com> Zip Code 80209 Select a Topic Other: Alameda
reduction Comments Please do not reduce the lanes on Alameda Boulevard - it is one of the only thoroughfares
through the central part of Denver -- we do drive cars, many of us, I know that the bike lobby is strong but
please listen to working class people who need to drive to their jobs and who don't want a massive traffic jam
clogging up Alameda. If you reduce the lanes you are going backwards - the traffic will remain but it is going to
be at a standstill during busy times. How does this help? We don't want a reduced lane with white poles -
enough already, stop meddling, the street is fine as it is. Please stop the Alameda street reduction. And another
thing, can you actually clean the streets that currently have all your little white plastic poles? Leaves and trash
pile up there and nobody cleans it, ever, only the bike lane on Marion gets cleaned because, you know, the bike
lobby is strong. Think of others in this city besides the privileged bicyclists. Thank you.

Hi Karen,
Yes. I have a few updates that were missed. * In our Safe and Slow Streets presentation, we indicated that 29th
Ave and Williams St were “Complete” as I understood this to be substantially complete. The website now says,
“Mostly complete with final items to be installed by end of summer 2025.” Any issues with just saying
“Complete?” Copying @Weis, Gavin<mailto:Gavin.Weis@denvergov.org> for insight. * The 2024 bike paving
project details all reference “DOTI is moving forward with a design…” Since these were already built, I think we
need to change the tense to be “DOTI constructed a bikeway on X street that included the following
elements:…” * 7th Ave: Mariposa Street to Delaware Street Project Status should match the timeline for the
other NTMP projects (“Installation planned for fall 2025”). And it needs a “Project Description: This
neighborhood bikeway will include pedestrian safety improvements such as curb extensions to shorten crossing
distances as well as traffic calming elements that slow down vehicle speeds such as speed cushions.” * W
Kentucky Ave needs a “Project Description: This project includes adding bikeway protection elements along the
Kentucky bike lane to prevent vehicle encroachment.” * W Ohio Ave needs a “Project Description: This project
includes adding a counterflow buffered bike lane to safely facilitate bike travel in the WB direction around
Huston Lake Park. It will also include pedestrian safety improvements such as curb extensions at intersections
and additional pedestrian crossing markings.” * We should add the Knox Ct Neighborhood Bikeway upgrades
from Kentucky to Alameda which are being carried forward through the Fall 2025 NTMP project. Details: *
Neighborhood: Westwood * Project Status: Installation planned for fall 2025 * Project Description: This
neighborhood bikeway upgrade will include pedestrian safety improvements such as pedestrian striping and
signage, traffic calming that will slow down vehicle speeds, and wayfinding signage to bring the neighborhood
bikeway up the DOTI’s latest bikeway standards. * Program: Neighborhood Transportation Management
Program (NTMP) * Market Street Status should show as Complete * GVR project status needs to be updated.
@Pogue, Eric<mailto:Eric.Pogue@denvergov.org> – Can you indicate when construction is slated to be
substantially complete? Also, this was not an NTMP project. Program would be Bond I believe? * Uvalda is all
complete I believe. @Vigil, Rachael<mailto:Rachael.Vigil@denvergov.org> can you confirm?
Thanks all!

to: Boncore, Brett - DOTI CE0403 Engineer-Architect Supervisor <Brett.Boncore@denvergov.org>; Price,
cc: Cieciek, Gregory M. - DOTI CE2294 Project Manager Engineering Senior
b:
Hi Brittany and Brett- Please find our response to Jason and the neighbors with the follow up information they
had asked for. Let me know if you are ok with us sending. Thanks! ---
Jason-
I wanted to follow up from our recent meeting. I know the neighbors had some questions that we promised to
follow up on. Please find our responses below:
Will this project cause an increase in congestion on side streets, like E Virginia Ave and E Bayaud Ave?
Based on DOTI's analysis, the impact to side streets will be minimal; no more than 10% of vehicles will reroute
throughout the entire day. A diversion analysis was conducted and anticipates that Virginia Avenue will
experience approximately 490 additional vehicles per day.
Crash Data (1/1/19- 7/31/24)- 144 Crashes over the most recent 5 year period
Although no pedestrian and bike facilities are present on Alameda, the improvements will improve safety where
pedestrian and bicycle facilities intersect with the corridor. This will make it easier and safer for all users to
reach destinations along Alameda regardless of mode choice.
Many of the questions that were asked, are also addressed on our website in an FAQ<https://www.denvergov.
org/Government/Agencies-Departments-Offices/Agencies-Departments-Offices-Directory/Department-of-Trans
portation-and-Infrastructure/Programs-Services/Projects>. Please let the neighbors know, we are always here
to answer any other questions they may have.
Thank you- Molly
Molly Lanphier, MPH | Community Designer Department of Transportation & Infrastructure | Transportation

<Gregory.Cieciek@denvergov.org> Subject: Alameda Jason Follow Up
Hi Brittany and Brett- Please find our response to Jason and the neighbors with the follow up information they
had asked for. Let me know if you are ok with us sending. Thanks! ---
Jason-
I wanted to follow up from our recent meeting. I know the neighbors had some questions that we promised to
follow up on. Please find our responses below:
Didn’t they also ask for the peak hour volumes? We can provide those if needed…
Will this project cause an increase in congestion on side streets, like E Virginia Ave and E Bayaud Ave?
Based on DOTI's analysis, the impact to side streets will be minimal; no more than 10% of vehicles will reroute
throughout the entire day. A diversion analysis was conducted and anticipates that Virginia Avenue will
experience approximately 490 additional vehicles per day. I am curious where this 490 number came from
(here and in the FAQs)… The latest diversion analysis I found anticipates a total of about ~575 veh/day (or 1-2
vehicles per minute, during the peak hour). Either way, if we keep 490 per day, we should also say “(or ~1
vehicle per minute, during the peak hour).”
Crash Data (1/1/19- 7/31/24)- 144 Crashes over the most recent 5 year period
Although no pedestrian and bike facilities are present on Alameda, the improvements will improve safety where
pedestrian and bicycle facilities intersect with the corridor. This will make it easier and safer for all users to
reach destinations along Alameda regardless of mode choice.
Many of the questions that were asked, are also addressed on our website in an FAQ<https://www.denvergov.
org/Government/Agencies-Departments-Offices/Agencies-Departments-Offices-Directory/Department-of-Trans
portation-and-Infrastructure/Programs-Services/Projects>. Please let the neighbors know, we are always here
to answer any other questions they may have.
Thank you- Molly

Hello, As our Alameda project is nearing kickoff, the neighbors have asked me to reach out again about a
problem patio on Alameda at Downing (District 6) at Olivia's. I've conversed with Gregory about this project and
patio, so that's why I am including him on this email. Please see the attached photos.
The patio at Olivia's is way too big (104"). The current sidewalk from the railing to curb is 56", but that sidewalk
amount includes the ROW. For comparison's sake, I have included a photo of the neighbor's patio at Starbucks,
which is 57". As you can see from the photo, Starbucks has a legal amount of sidewalk available, as well as a
29" ROW. The ROW at my house on 1101 E Alameda Ave, Denver, CO 80209 is about 1'.
Because of this huge patio, people have to walk single file on the sidewalk to avoid being hit by vehicle mirrors.
My husband has been hit in the shoulder by a mirror on a passing vehicle.
I'd really like to know more about the ROW size on E Alameda and Downing and the required amount of
sidewalk. I have time to meet in person if you like. From what I read online, the sidewalk is required to be at
least 36", but 56" less 12" for a minimum ROW is 34".
This patio is never used for seating by the restaurant. As a neighborhood, we'd all love to see the patio
removed.
Thank you for your time.
Shawnda Maher 303-253-5599 1101 E Alameda Ave

cc: "Cieciek; Gregory M. - DOTI CE2294 Project Manager Engineering Senior"
b:
Hi Again-
I found the ALR Diversion Diagram (attached) and it looks like the 490 number is just referring to Virginia, which
we do clarify below, but I do think the group’s question was broader than Virginia, in which case the 575 number
is best. I will reflect those changes below and keep it to vehicles per minute during peak hours.
I also found the latest traffic count done in September 2022. @Boncore, Brett - DOTI CE0403
Engineer-Architect Supervisor<mailto:Brett.Boncore@denvergov.org>, can you let me know which peak hour
volumes would be best to reference?
I forgot they asked about the speed limit, so will be including that as well. As of now, Alameda is 30mph and this
project will be reducing it to 25 mph.

f: 2025-07-11_16-17-18 - Alameda_ Email Follow Up to Jason and Neighbors.pdf
s: Alameda: Email Follow Up to Jason and Neighbors
to: "Holloway; Tykus R. - DOTI Deputy Manager" <Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org>; "Kuhn; Nancy - DOTI
cc: "Good; Karen A. - DOTI CE3476 Project Manager Engineering Supervisor"
b:
Hi All-
Please find below a draft of the email to be sent as a follow up to Jason concerning Alameda Lane
Repurposing. Please let me know if you have any issues. Tykus, let me know if you also want me to share with
Amy.
Once Brett is back in town, I am hoping to see what we can do from a design perspective and we can then
review next steps with Amy.
Thanks and have a great weekend! ----------- Message to be sent to Jason, which will be shared with Jill
Anschutz, Kimberly Kolesar, Cody, Belzley, Amy Guttmann, Illana Poley and Lisa Diedrich
I wanted to follow up from our recent meeting. We are taking the neighbors’ concerns very seriously and
assessing what we can do to address some of the concerns they brought up. There were also questions and
requests for information at the meeting that we promised to follow up on. Please find our responses below:
Will this project cause an increase in congestion on side streets, like E Virginia Ave and E Bayaud Ave?
Based on DOTI's analysis, the impact to side streets will be minimal; no more than 10% of vehicles will reroute
throughout the entire day. A diversion analysis was conducted and anticipates that Virginia Avenue will
experience approximately 490 additional vehicles per day, which translates to ~1-2 vehicles per minute.
Crash Data
We ran the crash data for the most recent five years (July 1, 2020 through July 3, 2025) and found there have
been 255 crashes which is up from the 192 (from 2018-2022) when we first assessed the corridor.
Peak Hour Volumes
Existing peak hour turning movement counts were conducted at all 16 study area intersections along Alameda
Avenue on Tuesday, September 20, 2022. The counts were conducted in 15-minute intervals during the
morning and afternoon peak hours of adjacent street traffic from 7:00 AM to 9:00 AM and 4:00 PM to 6:00 PM.
In addition, average daily traffic (ADT) volumes along three sections of Alameda Avenue were collected on the
same day as the peak hour counts with the exception of the Lincoln Street to Logan Street ADT volume.
I have attached the data associated with these traffic counts.
Speed Limit
The posted speed limit along Alameda Avenue is 30 miles per hour. The revised speed limit post-project
installation is planned to be 25 miles per hour. Any changes to the project scope could require a re-evaluation
of the speed limit change.
Many of the questions that were asked are also addressed on our website in an FAQ<https://www.denvergov.o
rg/Government/Agencies-Departments-Offices/Agencies-Departments-Offices-Directory/Department-of-Transp
ortation-and-Infrastructure/Programs-Services/Projects>. Please let the neighbors know that we are always
here to answer any other questions they may have.
Thank you- Molly
Molly Lanphier, MPH | Community Designer Department of Transportation & Infrastructure | Transportation
Implementation Division 720.913.4549 Phone | 720.619.1018 Cell

and Infrastructure <Amy.Ford@denvergov.org> Cc: Holloway, Tykus R. - DOTI Deputy Manager
<Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org>; Kuhn, Nancy - DOTI Marketing and Communications Director
<Nancy.Kuhn@denvergov.org>; Price, Brittany C. - DOTI CE2781 Engineer-Architect Manager
<Brittany.Price@denvergov.org>; Boncore, Brett - DOTI CE0403 Engineer-Architect Supervisor
<Brett.Boncore@denvergov.org>; Good, Karen A. - DOTI CE3476 Project Manager Engineering Supervisor
<Karen.Good@denvergov.org>; Cieciek, Gregory M. - DOTI Project Manager Engineering Senior
<Gregory.Cieciek@denvergov.org> Subject: For Your Review: Alameda Lane Repurposing Email to Neighbors
Hi Amy- We have a meeting tomorrow at 2 pm to discuss design options for Alameda, but I wanted to share the
below email we are hoping to send to Jason Gallardo and neighbors, as a follow up to our in person meeting a
couple weeks back. They had asked for some specific follow up. Let me know if you are ok with me sending or
if you have any questions or concerns.
Thank you! ------ Message to be sent to Jason, which will be shared with Jill Anschutz, Kimberly Kolesar, Cody,
Belzley, Amy Guttmann, Illana Poley and Lisa Diedrich
I wanted to follow up from our recent meeting. We are taking the neighbors’ concerns very seriously and
assessing what we can do to address some of the concerns they brought up. We are currently briefing DOTI
leadership and hope to share more about our next steps by the end of July. There were also questions and
requests for information at the meeting that we promised to follow up on. Please find our responses below:
Will this project cause an increase in congestion on side streets, like E Virginia Ave and E Bayaud Ave?
Based on DOTI's analysis, the impact to side streets will be minimal; no more than 10% of vehicles will reroute
throughout the entire day. A diversion analysis was conducted and anticipates that Virginia Avenue will
experience approximately 490 additional vehicles per day, which translates to ~1-2 vehicles per minute.
Crash Data
We ran the crash data for the most recent five years (July 1, 2020 through July 3, 2025) and found there have
been 255 crashes which is up from the 192 (from 2018-2022) when we first assessed the corridor.
Peak Hour Volumes
Existing peak hour turning movement counts were conducted at all 16 study area intersections along Alameda
Avenue on Tuesday, September 20, 2022. The counts were conducted in 15-minute intervals during the
morning and afternoon peak hours of adjacent street traffic from 7:00 AM to 9:00 AM and 4:00 PM to 6:00 PM.
In addition, average daily traffic (ADT) volumes along three sections of Alameda Avenue were collected on the
same day as the peak hour counts with the exception of the Lincoln Street to Logan Street ADT volume.
I have attached the data associated with these traffic counts.
Speed Limit
The posted speed limit along Alameda Avenue is 30 miles per hour. The revised speed limit post-project
installation is planned to be 25 miles per hour. Any changes to the project scope could require a re-evaluation
of the speed limit change.
Many of the questions that were asked are also addressed on our website in an FAQ<https://www.denvergov.o
rg/Government/Agencies-Departments-Offices/Agencies-Departments-Offices-Directory/Department-of-Transp
ortation-and-Infrastructure/Programs-Services/Projects>. Please let the neighbors know that we are always
here to answer any other questions they may have.
Thank you- Molly
