f: 2025-07-16_21-35-17 - _EXTERNAL_ Public Comment at Denver City Council Public Comm.pdf
s: Public Comment at Denver City Council Public Comment
to: mayorsoffice@denvergov.org; alan.salazar@denvergov.org; "Lanphier; Molly - DOTI CE2294 Project
b:
These remarks were made before the Denver City Council on July 14, 2025.
The residents of WIndsor Gardens wish that the Mayor would respond to our many requests for him to come
and talk to our residents about the Dayton Street Reconfiguration, and listen to our concerns about how the
street is being made less safe by the addition of parking on the East side of Dayton and the removal of the
center turn lane.
On April 22, close to 300 members of the Windsor Gardens Community gathered to listen to a presentation
regarding the Denver Office of Transportation and Infrastructure's proposed changes to South Dayton Street
from Alameda to Mississippi. The residents were horrified to hear that DoTI decided to remove the Center Turn
Lane and to allow parking on both the East and West side of Dayton. Over an hour of Windsor Gardens
residents concerns were disregarded by DoTI's Community Designer who presented the plan at Windsor
Gardens.
By removing the "Safety Lane" or Center Turn Lane, DoTI has once again shown its preference for parking over
people, parking over public safety.
Changing Dayton Street's configuration from 3 lanes to 2 lanes will cause an increase in both head on and rear
end collisions, as drivers are forced to cross the center line in an attempt to go around trash trucks which use
Dayton to collect large dumpsters from Windsor Gardens' buildings, drivers attempting to parallel park, people
darting out to cross the street between cars and drivers making any number of non marked left turns outside of
the proposed marked short turn lanes at East Center Avenue and Alton Street.
DoTI also has included a "pedestrian rescue island" that may obstruct and limit the ability of Windsor Gardens
residents to access one of our underground garages, which will impact over 300 residents.
District 5 Councilwoman Amanda Sawyer stated at the July 9, 2025 General Obligation Bond Working Group,
that, and I quote "I don't trust DoTI, I do not trust the Department of Transportation and Infrastructure to get this
done right."
Windsor Garden's residents feel the exact same way. We no longer trust DoTI to reconfigure Dayton Street so
that it is safe, and we certainly feel that DoTI would rather risk the safety of Windsor Gardens residents for
additional parking spaces along Dayton than to provide a safe street for all residents of the Windsor
Community.
Also on July 9th, Councilwoman Sawyer stated that "I am out of patience with this process, I am incredibly
frustrated and incredibly angry." The residents of Windsor Gardens feel exactly the same, especially as
Windsor Gardens was excluded from the initial Memorandum regarding the Dayton Street project.
The only individuals included in the initial Memorandum were the Park Forest HOA, DoTI, and Councilwoman
Sawyer, hiring Fehr & Peers to study traffic concerns on Dayton Street. Leaving out the voices of 3500
residents who use Dayton as 2 of 3 means of egress out of Windsor Gardens, was a major mistake and
unfortunately, the residents of Windsor Gardens are being put at risk by DoTI's dangerous and negligent plan.
We would ask the council to consider suspending or rescinding the South Dayton paving project until DoTI
creates a plan that does not put drivers from Windsor Gardens into dangerous situations as they exit and enter
the property.
Windsor Gardens residents may be over 55, but that does not mean we are expendable!
Christine Shock 9625 East Center Avenue #8D Denver, CO 80247
christine.shock@gmail.com<mailto:christine.shock@gmail.com>
__________________________________________
I am here today to discuss the proposed changes to the Dayton Street Repaving Project. This project was the
result of the parking issues on Dayton Street. DoTi proposed to eliminate the middle lane and allow parking on
both sides of Dayton.
You need that middle lane to allow the 3,301 Residents of Windsor Gardens, a 55+ Community, to make a left
turn when driving North on Dayton Street to obtain access to their residence. DoTI informed us that they are all
about Safety. By removing that middle lane, you are removing our safety and you will see accidents increase
and someone will be rear ended on a weekly basis.
Has anyone noticed that Alameda Ave. is now 35 mph, but most drivers still go 40+ to 45 the old speed limit.
Speeds on Dayton are
Are frequently the same as those (40+ to 45 mph) that occur on Alameda. By narrowing the road and removing
the turn lane DoTI feels they are creating a safer driving situation???
The changes proposed by DoTI will lead to a loss of life. Why are we waiting for this to occur before a real,
usable, solution is reached. Currently, we do not have that. I also do not feel that DoTI came to work with
Windsor Gardens residents. One meeting was at George Washington H.S. during a snow storm, and Windsor
Gardens Residents had to continuously ask at that meeting to meet with DoTI. The first time DoTI did come to
speak with us, they were not there to work with Windsor Gardens, but to dictate their final decision.
We, the residents, do not have a change that will work for residents who live on either side of Dayton Street.
Thank You,
Janis Mayer 615 S. Alton Way 4C Denver, CO

f: 2025-07-19_17-20-46 - _EXTERNAL_ Support for East Alameda Lane Repurposing project.pdf
s: Support for East Alameda Lane Repurposing project
b:
All,
I am a resident of Denver, living in the Wash park neighborhood. I have three young kids (ages 6, 3, 3), a dog
that we walk multiple times a day, and I am both a bicycle and auto commuter to my office in Cherry Creek. We
bike our kids to school 95% of the days to Steele elementary and St. John’s and have to cross Alameda Street
to do that. We walk our dog morning and night and my husband and I bike commute when weather allows us to
and we drive to the office as well. I wanted to paint this context as someone who is out in the streets and
impacted by alameda through multiple modes (walk, bike, drive both individually and with dependents)
I am an advocate of the safety improvements proposed on Alameda street and thank you for your work on this
project.
I recently got an email from our neighbors sharing this article for ActForAlameda.com which, in my opinion, is
spreading misinformation to neighbors, and based on a classic nimby fear based reaction. I am trying to counter
their efforts but am a busy working mom.
I wanted to reach out and let you know many neighbors DO support this. We want alameda safer. We want less
lanes. We want more crosswalks. We want a road diet and narrowing lanes which will slow traffic. We want
more friction so people pay attention when driving vs treating alameda as a highway.
But many of the neighbors who support it the most (working parents with kids) are so busy they never have the
time to get involved in these issues. So you typically only hear from the people who don’t support something.
Please know neighbors do support safe streets and support what you are doing. Road diets are a good thing!
I don’t have much time but if I can help I just wanted you to know.
Thank you for your efforts in this.
Liz Chester 245 South Williams Street Denver, CO 80209 503.706.3697
elizabeth.dilorenzo@gmail.com<mailto:elizabeth.dilorenzo@gmail.com>

to: "Williams; Nicholas - DOTI Deputy Manager Internal & External Affairs"
b:
Hello,
Thank you for your message. I will be out of the office from Sunday, July 14th through Thursday, July 25th, and
will return on Monday, July 28th. I will have very limited access to emails and messages.
For any project questions during this time, please reach out to the appropriate contact:
(cid:127) Alameda Lane Repurposing: Molly Lanphier –
molly.lanphier@denvergov.org<mailto:molly.lanphier@denvergov.org>
(cid:127) 5280 Trail – Acoma Street: Sam Beneck – sam.beneck@denvergov.org<mailto:sam.beneck@denvergov.org>
(cid:127) All other inquiries: Karen Good – karen.good@denvergov.org<mailto:karen.good@denvergov.org>
I’ll respond to your message as soon as possible upon my return.
Thanks, Greg

cc: "Holloway; Tykus R. - DOTI Deputy Manager" <Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org>; "Price; Brittany C. -
b:
Thank you, Molly,
I don’t see the attached file. Does it show where the counts come from, within the project being proposed or
alameda in general. Is this section of alameda considered to be a part of the high injury network? Thanks for
any info you can share.
Best,
Jason A. Gallardo J.D. Partner | MG Public Affairs www.mgpublicaffairs.com<https://www.mgpublicaffairs.com>
M: 970-366-1128 ________________________________ From: Lanphier, Molly - DOTI Project Manager
Engineering Senior <Molly.Lanphier@denvergov.org> Sent: Wednesday, July 16, 2025 4:56:28 PM To: Jason
Gallardo <jason@mgpublicaffairs.com> Cc: Holloway, Tykus R. - DOTI Deputy Manager
<Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org>; Price, Brittany C. - DOTI CE2781 Engineer-Architect Manager
<Brittany.Price@denvergov.org>; Cieciek, Gregory M. - DOTI Project Manager Engineering Senior
<Gregory.Cieciek@denvergov.org> Subject: Follow Up from Alameda Lane Repurposing
Hi Jason-
I wanted to follow up from our recent meeting. We are taking the neighbors’ concerns very seriously and
assessing what we can do to address some of the concerns they brought up. We are currently briefing DOTI
leadership and hope to share more about our next steps by the end of July. There were also questions and
requests for information at the meeting that we promised to follow up on. Please find our responses below:
Will this project cause an increase in congestion on side streets, like E Virginia Ave and E Bayaud Ave?
Based on DOTI's analysis, the impact to side streets will be minimal; no more than 10% of vehicles will reroute
throughout the entire day. A diversion analysis was conducted and anticipates that Virginia Avenue will
experience approximately 490 additional vehicles per day, which translates to ~1-2 vehicles per minute.
Crash Data
We ran the crash data for the most recent five years (July 1, 2020 through July 3, 2025) and found there have
been 255 crashes which is up from the 192 (from 2018-2022) when we first assessed the corridor.
Peak Hour Volumes
Existing peak hour turning movement counts were conducted at all 16 study area intersections along Alameda
Avenue on Tuesday, September 20, 2022. The counts were conducted in 15-minute intervals during the
morning and afternoon peak hours of adjacent street traffic from 7:00 AM to 9:00 AM and 4:00 PM to 6:00 PM.
In addition, average daily traffic (ADT) volumes along three sections of Alameda Avenue were collected on the
same day as the peak hour counts with the exception of the Lincoln Street to Logan Street ADT volume.
I have attached the data associated with these traffic counts.
Speed Limit
The posted speed limit along Alameda Avenue is 30 miles per hour. The revised speed limit post-project
installation is planned to be 25 miles per hour. Any changes to the project scope could require a re-evaluation
of the speed limit change.
Many of the questions that were asked are also addressed on our website in an FAQ<https://www.denvergov.o
rg/Government/Agencies-Departments-Offices/Agencies-Departments-Offices-Directory/Department-of-Transp
ortation-and-Infrastructure/Programs-Services/Projects>. Please let the neighbors know that we are always
here to answer any other questions they may have.
Thank you- Molly
Molly Lanphier, MPH | Community Designer Department of Transportation & Infrastructure | Transportation

b:

Subject: For Your Review: Alameda Lane Repurposing Email to Neighbors
Hi Amy- We have a meeting tomorrow at 2 pm to discuss design options for Alameda, but I wanted to share the
below email we are hoping to send to Jason Gallardo and neighbors, as a follow up to our in person meeting a
couple weeks back. They had asked for some specific follow up. Let me know if you are ok with me sending or
if you have any questions or concerns.
Thank you! ------ Message to be sent to Jason, which will be shared with Jill Anschutz, Kimberly Kolesar, Cody,
Belzley, Amy Guttmann, Illana Poley and Lisa Diedrich
I wanted to follow up from our recent meeting. We are taking the neighbors’ concerns very seriously and
assessing what we can do to address some of the concerns they brought up. We are currently briefing DOTI
leadership and hope to share more about our next steps by the end of July. There were also questions and
requests for information at the meeting that we promised to follow up on. Please find our responses below:
Will this project cause an increase in congestion on side streets, like E Virginia Ave and E Bayaud Ave?
Based on DOTI's analysis, the impact to side streets will be minimal; no more than 10% of vehicles will reroute
throughout the entire day. A diversion analysis was conducted and anticipates that Virginia Avenue will
experience approximately 490 additional vehicles per day, which translates to ~1-2 vehicles per minute.
Crash Data
We ran the crash data for the most recent five years (July 1, 2020 through July 3, 2025) and found there have
been 255 crashes which is up from the 192 (from 2018-2022) when we first assessed the corridor.
Peak Hour Volumes
Existing peak hour turning movement counts were conducted at all 16 study area intersections along Alameda
Avenue on Tuesday, September 20, 2022. The counts were conducted in 15-minute intervals during the
morning and afternoon peak hours of adjacent street traffic from 7:00 AM to 9:00 AM and 4:00 PM to 6:00 PM.
In addition, average daily traffic (ADT) volumes along three sections of Alameda Avenue were collected on the
same day as the peak hour counts with the exception of the Lincoln Street to Logan Street ADT volume.
I have attached the data associated with these traffic counts.
Speed Limit
The posted speed limit along Alameda Avenue is 30 miles per hour. The revised speed limit post-project
installation is planned to be 25 miles per hour. Any changes to the project scope could require a re-evaluation
of the speed limit change.
Many of the questions that were asked are also addressed on our website in an FAQ<https://www.denvergov.o
rg/Government/Agencies-Departments-Offices/Agencies-Departments-Offices-Directory/Department-of-Transp
ortation-and-Infrastructure/Programs-Services/Projects>. Please let the neighbors know that we are always
here to answer any other questions they may have.
Thank you- Molly

b:
Thanks, Amy. Also, wanted to address Mark Samuelson’s second question about the project a dozen years
ago. We have a blurb on the FAQs regarding that:
Am I having déjà vu? Wasn't a lane reduction attempted on Alameda several years ago? DOTI installed a lane
reduction on Alameda back in 2008-2010 timeframe. In the eastbound direction, there was a lane drop at
Downing necessitating a lane merge before Downing which resulted in stacking of vehicles on the eastbound
approach to Downing Street with one lane fully queued and the other lane free flowing. During this time the
Alameda average daily traffic was over 20,000. It is now much lower, so Alameda is a better candidate for a
lane reduction.
Let me know if you need any more talking points/ help to get back to Mark, Nancy!
Thanks! Molly

From: Kuhn, Nancy - DOTI Marketing and Communications Director Sent: Thursday, July 24, 2025 9:31 AM
Subject: RE: Alameda project follow-up...

what are access management safety enhancements?

Mark,
Our plan is to move forward with safety improvements on Alameda Avenue, making the corridor safer for all
users. The work planned includes: (cid:127) Reduced speed limit to 25 mph (cid:127) New designated turn lanes at eight
intersections (Pennsylvania St., Pearl St., Washington St., Clarkson St., Emerson St., Ogden St., Lafayette St.,
and Humboldt St.) (cid:127) Painted pedestrian bulb outs at every intersection between Pearl St. and Franklin St. (11
intersections) (cid:127) Access management safety enhancements at Grant St, Pennsylvania St, Corona St., and
Franklin St. (cid:127) New striped medians and pedestrian refuge islands at these locations: Washington St.
intersection (west side); Emerson St. intersection (east side); Downing, Marion, and Corona street intersections
(east and west sides). (cid:127) Installation of a Rectangular Rapid Flashing Beacon, pedestrian refuge island, and
marked crosswalks at Franklin (note: this RRFB is part of separate plans and is likely to be installed first and
separately from the rest of the improvements) (cid:127) New marked crosswalks at Grant St. (cid:127) New School Zone
signage and markings east of Broadway, east of Logan St. and east of Humboldt St. (cid:127) Additional on-street
parking east of Pearl St. (cid:127) Protected bike lane approaches and green crossing markings at Washington St. and
Emerson St.
We don’t have an exact date yet for implementation. We’ve been giving travel patterns a little time to adjust to
the Alameda underpass project’s temporary lane closures happening between Bannock and Santa Fe, and
have been affording time to consider and address new and lingering community concerns about the plan for the
lane repurposing.
Some of the data we’ve collected that speak to the need for the project include:
Crash data We gathered crash data for the most recent five years (July 1, 2020 through July 3, 2025) and found
there have been 255 crashes which is up from the 192 (from 2018-2022) when we first assessed the corridor.
Safety improvements for drivers have been recommended in alignment with the crash data (attached).
Traffic Speed and Volume data The posted speed limit along this segment of Alameda Avenue is 30 miles per
hour. 2024 speed data shows that speeding is an issue along this corridor (with 85th percentile speed = 38
mph). Following installation of our safety improvements, the posted speed limit is planned to be 25 miles per
hour.
We’ve collected average daily traffic volumes to predict potential diversion to side streets as a result of the lane
repurposing work. 2024 counts showed the average daily traffic volumes of Alameda Ave, west of Downing, to
be around 14,800 vehicles per day. * DOTI believes no more than 10% of vehicles total will reroute to side
streets throughout the day. * A diversion analysis conducted for Virginia Avenue predicts approximately 490
additional vehicles per day, which translates to about 1-2 vehicles per minute.
Average daily vehicle volumes on parallel east/west roads include: * Virginia between Logan and Downing = ~
4,200 vehicles a day * 1st Avenue (between Logan and Downing) = ~2,600 vehicles per day * 1st
Avenue/Speer (between Downing and University) = ~50,000 vehicles per day
Wanted to mention that many of the questions that have been asked about the project are addressed on our
website in an FAQ<https://www.denvergov.org/Government/Agencies-Departments-Offices/Agencies-Departm
ents-Offices-Directory/Department-of-Transportation-and-Infrastructure/Programs-Services/Projects>.
Per your question about a similar, past project, DOTI installed a lane reduction on Alameda back in the
2008-2010 timeframe. In the eastbound direction, there was a lane drop at Downing necessitating a lane merge
before Downing. This merge resulted in the stacking of vehicles on the eastbound approach to Downing Street
with one lane fully queued and the other lane free flowing. At that time, the Alameda average daily traffic was
over 20,000. What would be different now is that the average daily traffic is much lower. Alameda is a better
candidate now for a lane reduction.
Sincerely, Nancy

f: 2025-07-25_16-15-44 - RE_ D8 CM Lewis SPEED Program Briefing.pdf
s: RE: D8 CM Lewis SPEED Program Briefing
to: "Lewis; Shontel M. - CC Member District 8 Denver City Council" <Shontel.Lewis@denvergov.org>; "Carey;
cc: "Hudson; Tymber - CC Marketing and Communications Staff" <Tymber.Hudson@denvergov.org>;
b:
Hello Councilmember Lewis and Jesse,
Thank you both for your time and questions earlier this week discussing DOTI’s SPEED program. Attached are
the briefing slides as well as program collateral.
Also attached is the equity and mitigation report developed by DOTI and OSEI summarizing potential risks and
associated mitigations to protect marginalized communities from unnecessary hardships caused by safety
speed cameras. DOTI and OSEI have developed an action plan to track the implementation of developed
mitigation strategies to ensure program success and community transparency.
We also have some follow up information for you related to your question about the data used to develop the
local, regional, and short trip assessments for the Federal and Alameda corridors. 1. The DOTI travel demand
model is based on DRCOG's regional travel demand model. DRCOG compiles and aggregates socio-economic
and travel diary data from household travel surveys, RTD surveys, and census data sources. Individual
people's locations are not tracked unless they willingly choose to participate in the household travel survey. 2.
The data is then cleaned, aggregated, and validated to ensure it replicates regional demographic, land use, and
traffic data. The data is updated every 5 years or so to reflect changing trends (such as work from home,
changes in RTD's transit service etc.). 3. The travel diary data requested by DRCOG is very detailed and
includes all kinds of information about a person's typical day - how many trips they took, how did they travel,
what was the trip purpose, what time did they leave, what route did they use etc. More technical details are
explained here - https://www.drcog.org/data-maps-modeling/data-modeling.
A quick reminder, we have no Council action items scheduled at this time, but I will let you know when that
changes.
Let us know if you have follow up questions or would like to schedule a future briefing to dive into any additional
details with the DOTI and OSEI team.
We will also be sharing a social media toolkit with Councilmembers to amplify safety messaging through social
media and newsletters throughout the fall.
Alaina McWhorter, MPA, CNP | Strategic Advisor Transportation & Infrastructure | City and County of Denver
alaina.mcwhorter@denvergov.org<mailto:alaina.mcwhorter@denvergov.org> Pronouns | She/Her/Hers

-----Original Appointment----- From: McWhorter, Alaina - DOTI Strategic Advisor Sent: Monday, May 12, 2025
1:00 PM To: McWhorter, Alaina - DOTI Strategic Advisor; DOTI Ordinance; Lewis, Shontel M. - CC Member
District 8 Denver City Council; Carey, Jesse - CC City Council Aide Senior; Decker, Jay - DOTI CE2159 City
Planner Principal; Eisinger, John R. - DOTI CE2777 Manager Engineering and Science; Calderon, Jessica M. -
OSEI Director of Operations and Innovation; Bryant, Jamila N. - OSEI CA2308 Administrator II;
Smith-Coronado, Mayra - OSEI Senior Racial Equity Data Analyst; Hillhouse, Jennifer L. - DOTI City Planning
Director; Gilbert, Kayla M. - DOTI Administrator II Cc: Hudson, Tymber - CC Marketing and Communications
Staff; MO Legislative Affairs; Tonikian, Victoria - MO Deputy Legislative Director Subject: D8 CM Lewis SPEED
Program Briefing When: Wednesday, July 23, 2025 3:15 PM-4:00 PM (UTC-07:00) Mountain Time (US &
Canada). Where: Microsoft Teams Meeting; 3:00 PM
DOTI’s SPEED program – 2024 Accomplishments and 2025 look ahead This is DOTI’s new program
announced with the Mayor and City Council this past June. The program increases safety by reducing speeds
through several targeted actions on Federal Boulevard and Alameda Avenue. These two corridors are a part of
the city’s high injury network – the streets where most serious and fatal injury crashes are occurring – and will
see new safety improvements implemented in 2025 aimed at reducing fatalities as Denver moves toward its
goal of zero fatalities by 2030. * Saturating corridors with speed limit signs and speed feedback signs, providing
visual reminders of posted limits; Start directed marketing program to encourage drivers to slow down *
Programming traffic signals to slow vehicles down to the posted limits * Engineering signal timing to prevent
excessive speeding at night by having more signals rest in red until a car arrives * Expanding use of signal
timing that gives pedestrians a head start crossing the street before the light turns * Deploying speed
enforcement on both corridors including surging mobile speed vans and piloting automated speed enforcement
cameras * green * Deploying speed enforcement on both corridors including surging mobile speed vans and
piloting automated speed enforcement cameras

From: Mallory Huggins <mallory.huggins@gmail.com> Sent: Monday, July 28, 2025 8:41 AM To: Cieciek,
Gregory M. - DOTI Project Manager Engineering Senior <Gregory.Cieciek@denvergov.org> Subject:
[EXTERNAL] Alameda Road Diet / Gazette article
Hi Greg,
My neighbors and I are very excited about the Alameda Road Diet project, and were frustrated by the Denver
Gazette article<https:/denvergazette.com/news/wash-park-residents-upset-alameda-avenue-construction-proje
ct/article_0840d019-ac7a-4928-bab5-42ac2c574907.html> from last week spotlighting neighbors who are
petitioning against the project. I am reaching out to document my support for the project, as someone who
walks, bikes, and drives around this neighborhood and finds Alameda to be a street in desperate need of
reconfiguration, and to see if you have any advice on how my neighbors and I can best support the project. I
know the project is already approved and in the works, but I also know that angry residents can have a lot of
power and I want to make sure it's clear to the right folks that they do not speak for the neighborhood.
Thanks for any advice you can provide! Mallory
-- Mallory Huggins (303) 947-7013
Pronouns: she/her

b:
Get Outlook for iOS<https://aka.ms/o0ukef> ________________________________ From: Garrett Fardon
Personal <garrett.fardon@gmail.com> Sent: Monday, July 28, 2025 4:06:32 PM To: Ford, Amy - DOTI HE3258
Manager Department of Transportation and Infrastructure <Amy.Ford@denvergov.org> Subject: [EXTERNAL]
Alameda Road Diet
Hi, I'm a resident of Platt Park who commutes along Downing and Alameda regularly. I'm really excited about
the proposed road diet along Alameda to calm traffic and to make it a safer experience when crossing the road
as a driver, pedestrian, and cyclist. I recently saw that a group of residents and business owners in the nearby
affluent neighborhood are opposing this proven, reasonable safety intervention over fears of bollards
(https://www.actforalameda.com<https://www.actforalameda.com>). I'm writing to beg the office to please not
backslide on this project- as someone who tries to use non-car modes of transportation I've seen a great deal of
projects aimed at making streets safer for road users other than drivers be eliminated over unproven concerns
from a few residents or business owners. Please listen to the traffic experts and not cave to a few rich
constituents that are fear mongering over the idea of bollards. Thank you
-Garrett Fardon (he/his)

Greg,
I’m not sure how aware you are of the growing concern & opposition from neighbors regarding the E Alameda
Lane Reconfiguration. There’s been a couple articles in the Denver Gazette<https:/denvergazette.com/news/w
ash-park-residents-upset-alameda-avenue-construction-project/article_0840d019-ac7a-4928-bab5-42ac2c574
907.html> recently along with a website<https:/www.actforalameda.com/> about this. A supportive
website<https:/safer-alameda.com> was also just created by other advocates.
I have arranged to meet with some of the neighbors concerned about the project on Tuesday, August 12 at 4pm
at Blackbird Public House at Alameda & Downing. I’m also opening it up to leaders & neighbors in WWPNA. It’d
be great if you could join us with any documentation that can further explain the project & impact expected to
the adjacent streets, as this seems to be a concern DOTI has not addressed yet, at least not in public.
Please review the article & websites for concerns from the neighbors. While the focus has been very narrowly
limited to Alameda & adjacent properties, it’s important that we look at & address the other issues that are
present nearby both currently existing & expected from the project. My reading is that the intersections of
Downing at Cedar, Dakota & Virginia are of the greatest concern, beyond the traffic volume changes to the e-w
parallel streets.
I’m also inviting council members from districts 6 & 7 to join so they have an opportunity to learn about the
neighbors concerns. Please confirm if you or an engineer familiar with the project can join us, not just Nancy
Kuhn.

to: "Johnson; Jon C. - DOTI CE2159 City Planner Principal" <Jonathan.Johnson@denvergov.org>; "Lanphier;
cc: "Eisinger; John R. - DOTI CE2777 Manager Engineering and Science" <John.Eisinger@denvergov.org>;
b:
Hi Jon,
Let me know if this works:
Thank you for taking the time to share your concerns regarding the Alameda Lane Repurposing project.
To clarify, the project does not involve reducing Alameda Avenue from four lanes to two. Rather, the proposed
design would convert the existing four-lanes to three lanes with a center turn lane at many intersections along
the corridor. This configuration has been shown nationwide to reduce traffic accidents while maintaining
reasonable traffic flow.
The project will also not severely impact access to business along the corridor. The goal for this project is to
strike a balance—improving safety for all users while maintaining vehicle access to residential areas and
businesses along the corridor.
Most importantly, the project does not include adding bike lanes along Alameda itself. Instead, it focuses on
improving the safety and visibility of north–south bike and pedestrian crossings of Alameda. These
enhancements aim to make it safer for people walking and biking across the corridor.
Thanks, Greg
Greg Cieciek, PLA | Community Designer Department of Transportation & Infrastructure | Transportation

Hi Molly & Greg,
I have a 311 case in my queue to resolve in the next few business days related to Alameda. Brett let me know
the two of you are running point on PI for this project. Can you help me draft a response to this 311 case so that
I can close it? I think it should be a a pretty easy response since most of the complaint/concern is entirely
inaccurate (bike lanes).
Customer message: "I read an article in Westword magazine 7/25/25 that stated that the City of Denver will
finalize a plan to reduce East Alameda Ave from 4 to 2 lanes and add bike lanes. This is another example of
Denver strangling business on the major thoroughfares. The general public cannot access local businesses due
to congestion caused by limiting car lanes to the point that the frustration builds to where people will longer
commute to Denver due to the hassle and congestion. The businesses have already expressed concern to the
City and the City does seem to care. Not everyone can ride a bike or wants to ride a bike to shop and for meals.
Denver, like all western US cities were built around the car, this is another example along with the many
reasons to leave Denver."
Let me know if I can assist in anyway related to this. I am nearing completion of a thorough crash analysis on
behalf of the design team on a per-intersection basis if that would also be helpful to see.
Thanks!
Best,

Council members Kashmann & Alvidrez,
I am forwarding you a message I sent to Greg Cieciek at DOTI concerning an opportunity to meet with
neighbors concerned about the Alameda Lane Repurposing. I know both of your offices have been contacted
by concerned residents, including creating a website that states concerns about distribution of traffic onto
parallel neighborhood streets.
In order to fill in the gaps of understanding about the project & timing (fluid due to SPEED initiative), I have
arranged to meet with some of the neighbors who created the website. We will be meeting on Tuesday, August
12 at 4pm at Blackbird Public House. I request that you or one of your staff be present with us as we discuss
this project & the issues that need to be addressed but have not been, at least not publicly by DOTI.
I’m also opening this up to WWPNA leaders & neighbors as it’s been over a year since the latest display was
open to the public for comment. My goal is to have a civil discussion about the project, how it’s intended to
resolve issues identified for prevention, not just reaction, and to consider solutions to the expected &
planned-for additional pass-through traffic flow onto neighborhood streets. Your participation will send a strong
message about your support for residents & their concerns as changes continue to be made to our
neighborhoods.
Since the article was published, an additional website<https://safer-alameda.com> in support of the project has
been created. While not by neighbors of the neighborhoods, it reflects support of many who do reside or pass
through our neighborhoods.
Please respond with who will be available to be present to represent your office.
Civically,
Thomas Topero c/txt: 720.468.0022 Thomas.Topero@gmail.com LinkedIn/Bluesky: topero

2025 9:47:13 AM To: Ford, Amy - DOTI HE3258 Manager Department of Transportation and Infrastructure
<Amy.Ford@denvergov.org> Subject: FW: [EXTERNAL] Constituent concerns re: proposed changes to
Alameda Ave
Hi Amy, Just received this. presume you’ve received as well? Pls advise on next steps/response etc.
Jeff
From: Jill Anschutz <jill.hamilton@mac.com> Sent: Thursday, July 31, 2025 9:37 AM To:
mayor@denvergov.org Cc: Dolan, Jeff J. - MO Chief Strategy Officer and Counsel - Mayor's Office
<Jeff.Dolan@denvergov.org> Subject: [EXTERNAL] Constituent concerns re: proposed changes to Alameda
Ave
Dear Mayor Johnston,
On behalf of over 300 concerned residents and business owners along the Alameda corridor, we are reaching
out to express serious concerns about the proposed changes to Alameda Avenue in the Wash Park area.
While we understand the goal of improving safety and traffic flow, our community strongly believes that
removing a travel lane to add turn lanes will:
* Increase congestion and backups on Alameda, * Push frustrated drivers into nearby residential streets,
including Virginia Avenue, creating new safety hazards, * Harm the accessibility and economic vitality of our
local businesses, and * Require future, reactive interventions due to unintended traffic and safety impacts.
Attached to this email, you will find:
1. A formal letter outlining our concerns in detail, 2. Over 300 resident and business owner signatures in
opposition to the project, and 3. A recent Denver Gazette article highlighting the issue. We respectfully request
that the City pause this project and engage with our community to find a safer, more effective solution before
proceeding.
Thank you for your attention to this matter.
Sincerely,
Jill Anschutz 502.938.2947

Hi Amy, Just received this. presume you’ve received as well? Pls advise on next steps/response etc.
Jeff
From: Jill Anschutz <jill.hamilton@mac.com> Sent: Thursday, July 31, 2025 9:37 AM To:
mayor@denvergov.org Cc: Dolan, Jeff J. - MO Chief Strategy Officer and Counsel - Mayor's Office
<Jeff.Dolan@denvergov.org> Subject: [EXTERNAL] Constituent concerns re: proposed changes to Alameda
Ave
Dear Mayor Johnston,
On behalf of over 300 concerned residents and business owners along the Alameda corridor, we are reaching
out to express serious concerns about the proposed changes to Alameda Avenue in the Wash Park area.
While we understand the goal of improving safety and traffic flow, our community strongly believes that
removing a travel lane to add turn lanes will:
* Increase congestion and backups on Alameda, * Push frustrated drivers into nearby residential streets,
including Virginia Avenue, creating new safety hazards, * Harm the accessibility and economic vitality of our
local businesses, and * Require future, reactive interventions due to unintended traffic and safety impacts.
Attached to this email, you will find:
1. A formal letter outlining our concerns in detail, 2. Over 300 resident and business owner signatures in
opposition to the project, and 3. A recent Denver Gazette article highlighting the issue. We respectfully request
that the City pause this project and engage with our community to find a safer, more effective solution before
proceeding.
Thank you for your attention to this matter.
Sincerely,
Jill Anschutz 502.938.2947

Hello Mayor Johnston, Councilmember Kashmann, and Council Member Alvidrez, and DOTI,
I’m hoping to get a response to the story below for a story we are working on today.
A group called Act for Alameda is circulating a petition expressing concerns about proposed changes to
Alameda Avenue near Virginia Avenue.
Here is a snippet from the petition.
We, the undersigned residents and business owners of the neighborhoods and commercial corridors along
Alameda Avenue, are writing to express serious concerns regarding the proposed roadway redesign. The
changes — particularly the conversion of travel lanes into a turn lane — threaten to create substantial and
lasting negative impacts on traffic safety, neighborhood livability, and the vitality of local businesses.
Link to petition: https://www.actforalameda.com/letter-to-the-denver-mayor<https:/www.actforalameda.com/lett
er-to-the-denver-mayor>
If any of you are available for an on-camera interview at your earliest convenience, please let me know.
We would also consider inlcuding any written responses you may have as well.
The story is scheduled to begin airing at 4pm this afternoon.
Thanks,

f: 2025-08-06_13-14-57 - RE_ Group Upset over proposed changes to Alameda.pdf
s: RE: Group Upset over proposed changes to Alameda
cc: Mayor's Communications Office <MOComms@denvergov.org>; "Alvidrez; Flor C. - CC Member District 7
b:
Vicente,
Providing you this overview of the E. Alameda Avenue safety project between Franklin and Lincoln streets and
status.
Project overview The plan is to move forward with safety improvements on Alameda Avenue, making the
corridor safer for all users.
The work planned includes: * New designated turn lanes at eight intersections (Pennsylvania St., Pearl St.,
Washington St., Clarkson St., Emerson St., Ogden St., Lafayette St., and Humboldt St.) * Painted pedestrian
bulb outs at every intersection between Pearl St. and Franklin St. (11 intersections) * New striped medians and
pedestrian refuge islands at these locations: Washington St. intersection (west side); Emerson St. intersection
(east side); Downing, Marion, and Corona street intersections (east and west sides). * Reduced speed limit to
25 mph * Installation of a Rectangular Rapid Flashing Beacon, pedestrian refuge island, and marked
crosswalks at Franklin (note: this RRFB is part of separate plans and is likely to be installed first and separately
from the rest of the improvements) * New marked crosswalks at Grant St. * New School Zone signage and
markings east of Broadway, east of Logan St. and east of Humboldt St. * Additional on-street parking east of
Pearl St. * Protected bike lane approaches and green crossing markings at Washington St. and Emerson St.
Status We don’t have an exact date yet for implementation. We’ve been giving travel patterns a little time to
adjust to temporary lane closures happening a little west of there, between Bannock Street and Santa Fe Drive,
for the Alameda underpass project, and have been affording time to consider and address new and lingering
community concerns about the plan for the lane repurposing.
Project need: Some of the data we’ve collected that speak to the need for the project include:
Crash data We gathered crash data for the most recent five years (July 1, 2020 through July 3, 2025) and found
there have been 255 crashes which is up from the 192 (from 2018-2022) when we first assessed the corridor.
Safety improvements for drivers have been recommended in alignment with the crash data (attached).
Traffic Speed and Volume data The posted speed limit along this segment of Alameda Avenue is 30 miles per
hour. 2024 speed data shows that speeding is an issue along this corridor (with 85th percentile speed = 38
mph). Following installation of our safety improvements, the posted speed limit is planned to be 25 miles per
hour.
We’ve collected average daily traffic volumes to predict potential diversion to side streets as a result of the lane
repurposing work. 2024 counts showed the average daily traffic volumes of Alameda Ave, west of Downing, to
be around 14,800 vehicles per day. * DOTI believes no more than 10% of vehicles total will reroute to side
streets throughout the day. * A diversion analysis conducted for Virginia Avenue predicts approximately 490
additional vehicles per day, which translates to about 1-2 vehicles per minute.
Average daily vehicle volumes on parallel east/west roads include: * Virginia between Logan and Downing = ~
4,200 vehicles a day * 1st Avenue (between Logan and Downing) = ~2,600 vehicles per day * 1st
Avenue/Speer (between Downing and University) = ~50,000 vehicles per day
Wanted to mention that many of the questions that have been asked about the project are addressed on our
website in an FAQ<https://www.denvergov.org/Government/Agencies-Departments-Offices/Agencies-Departm
ents-Offices-Directory/Department-of-Transportation-and-Infrastructure/Programs-Services/Projects>.
Hope this is helpful.
Sincerely, Nancy
NANCY KUHN| Director City & County of Denver Department of Transportation & Infrastructure |
Communications Division Phone: 303-513-6046
nancy.kuhn@denvergov.org<mailto:nancy.kuhn@denvergov.org>
From: DOTI Communications Sent: Wednesday, August 6, 2025 11:54 AM To: Vicente Arenas
<Vicente.Arenas@kdvr.com>; Mayor's Communications Office <MOComms@denvergov.org>; Alvidrez, Flor C.
- CC Member District 7 Denver City Council <Flor.Alvidrez@denvergov.org>; Kashmann, Paul J. - CC Member
District 6 Denver City Council <Paul.Kashmann@denvergov.org>; DOTI Communications
<PW.Comms@denvergov.org> Subject: RE: Group Upset over proposed changes to Alameda
Vicente, Will send you some info shortly. Will be back at my computer in a few minutes. Sincerely, Nancy
NANCY KUHN| Director City & County of Denver Department of Transportation & Infrastructure |
Communications Division Phone: 303-513-6046
nancy.kuhn@denvergov.org<mailto:nancy.kuhn@denvergov.org>

From: Cieciek, Gregory M. - DOTI Project Manager Engineering Senior Sent: Wednesday, August 6, 2025
12:34 PM To: Thomas Topero <thomas.topero@gmail.com> Cc: molly.lanphier@denvergov.org Subject: RE:
[EXTERNAL] Alameda lane reconfiguration
Hi Thomas,
Thank you for reaching out and for organizing a meeting for neighbors to share their perspective views on the
Alameda Lane Repurposing Project. We appreciate your continued engagement and advocacy on behalf of the
community.
We are aware of both the growing opposition and the support the project has received. As with any proposed
changes to a major corridor, it’s understandable that there are a range of opinions, and we’re committed to
considering input from all voices.
We’re also currently reviewing our data collection with the latest numbers to ensure we’ve thoroughly examined
traffic volumes, crash history, and potential impacts to nearby intersections and adjacent streets, including
those you mentioned like Downing at Cedar, Dakota, and Virginia. Unfortunately, we will not have this analysis
and data available before the meeting, so we will not be able to attend the meeting on August 12, but we truly
value the dialogue that’s happening and want to continue gathering feedback from all sides.
We’ll be sure to share additional information as the project continues to develop and will continue to look for
opportunities to connect with community and neighborhood groups. Please don’t hesitate to share any notes or
follow-up questions that come out of your meeting—we’d be happy to review and respond where we can.
Thanks again for your outreach and for keeping us in the loop.
Best regards, Greg

f: 2025-08-14_14-27-32 - RE_ Follow Up from Alameda Project (5).pdf
s: RE: Follow Up from Alameda Project
to: "Lanphier; Molly - DOTI Project Manager Engineering Senior" <Molly.Lanphier@denvergov.org>;
cc: "Price; Brittany C. - DOTI CE2783 Engineer-Architect Director" <Brittany.Price@denvergov.org>; "Cieciek;
b:
Hi Molly and team,
Great job with the presentation this morning. Very glad to see we got a resolution and next steps moving
forward.
On item #2, @Marfitano, Steven C. –<mailto:Steven.Marfitano@denvergov.org> I can connect you to the KHA
(design) team, but can you take the lead from there on coordinating with KHA on an estimate for the redesign?
And on item #3, I think it would be appropriate to start diving into some of those Downing/Virginia safety and
operational enhancements we could make at this intersection to mitigate potential impacts of the diversion.
Based on our conversation earlier today, I think the potential solutions are self-evident (ped recall, bulbouts to
prevent illegal passing around LT vehicles, etc.) but we really need to have these designed and approved by
TME and others. It probably makes sense to add this task to the upcoming KHA change order as well.
@Lanphier, Molly –<mailto:Molly.Lanphier@denvergov.org> I will send this email to KHA today so that we can
hopefully have a cost estimate in the next few weeks. Let me (and Steven) get a feel from KHA on timeline and
we will get back to you.
Let me know if you have any other questions as we move forward!
Thanks again all,
Brett Boncore, PE| Engineering Supervisor City & County of Denver Department of Transportation &
Infrastructure | Transportation Design Phone: 720.865.3214
brett.boncore@denvergov.org<mailto:brett.boncore@denvergov.org>

cc: "Marfitano; Steven C. - DOTI CE0397 Engineer" <Steven.Marfitano@denvergov.org>
b:
Hi Matt,
I hope this week back from PNW-vacay hasn’t been too depressing n! Wanted to reach out to you regarding
the Alameda Lane Reduction project. We have been working with leadership to triage some high level
community and leadership concerns with constructing the project and recently received clear direction on a
path forward. After evaluating different options for moving forward in light of community and leadership
concerns, we have decided to move forward with a partial lane reduction (i.e. lane reduction only in the WB
direction). This will constitute a redesign of the project but aligns with the initial idea that we started this project
with.
I am copying one of our bright new engineer PMs, Steven Marfitano, who will be stepping in to manage this
project from the city side. Could you both find a time for us to discuss these changes, the impacts to TO budget
and schedule, and next steps?
I am sure there are a lot of questions we can discuss during this meeting but let us know if you have any key
questions that come up in the meantime. Thanks!
Brett Boncore, PE| Engineering Supervisor City & County of Denver Department of Transportation &
Infrastructure | Transportation Design Phone: 720.865.3214
brett.boncore@denvergov.org<mailto:brett.boncore@denvergov.org>
[cid:image001.png@01DC0D28.74077150]<https://www.denvergov.org/content/denvergov/en.html>

Dear Amy Ford
We are writing to express serious concerns regarding the proposed roadway redesign. The changes —
particularly the conversion of travel lanes into a turn lane — threaten to create substantial and lasting negative
impacts on traffic safety, neighborhood livability, and the vitality of local businesses.
Our key concerns are:
1. Increased congestion and traffic flow issues
Reducing Alameda’s travel lanes to add a dedicated turn lane will shrink roadway capacity and cause severe
backups, particularly during peak hours. This bottleneck will create gridlock and slow traffic along a major
east-west corridor.
2. Negative changes in driver behavior
Frustrated drivers will seek alternate routes through residential neighborhoods, leading to an increase in
cut-through traffic. Over time, this shift in driver habits could become permanent, burdening roads that were
never designed to serve as arterial routes.
3. Risk to Virginia Avenue and park-adjacent areas
Virginia Avenue, which borders Wash Park and community gathering areas, is particularly vulnerable.
Redirected traffic will put pedestrians, cyclists, and families at risk, turning a recreational corridor into a
high-traffic danger zone.
4. Neighborhood safety degradation
Residents who rely on Alameda for daily access will face greater difficulty entering and exiting their
neighborhoods. More traffic will be diverted onto smaller residential streets, increasing the risk of collisions,
speeding, and unsafe conditions for children, pedestrians, and pets.
5. Economic harm to local businesses
Businesses along Alameda depend on accessible, predictable traffic patterns. Increased congestion, confusion,
and delays may deter customers, reduce visibility, and undercut foot traffic — threatening the health of small
businesses that are vital to our community.
6. Lack of justifying safety data
Current traffic and safety data does not support the need for a major redesign. These changes appear to be
driven more by theoretical modeling than by demonstrated issues on the ground.
7. Need for transparent, collaborative planning
We urge the City to pause the project and commit to a broader, more inclusive public process. Community
voices have not been adequately engaged in a change that will deeply impact our quality of life and daily
mobility.
We appreciate your leadership and ask that you work with DOTI to reconsider the scope and design of this
project. We welcome the opportunity to meet with you and your staff to offer community-driven input that
prioritizes both safety and common-sense transportation planning.
Thank you for your time and commitment to a safer, more inclusive Denver.
Respectfully,
Raymond & Robin Morgan
480 S Marion Pkwy 1103A
Denver, CO 80209

f: 2025-08-25_14-16-55 - _EXTERNAL_ 182 signatures - Community Letter of Support for.pdf
s: 182 signatures - Community Letter of Support for the
cc: flor.alvidrez@denvergov.org
b:
Dear Mayor Johnston, Ms. Ford, and DOTI Representatives:
Preface: This letter represents an organic grassroots effort organized by David Hawkins, a local father
concerned about traffic safety in our neighborhood, neighbors, and community members. The signatures were
collected through our community website safer-alameda.com<http://safer-alameda.com>, where neighbors
could learn about the project and add their support. Over several weeks we have gathered 182 signatures from
community members who support this important safety initiative.
We, the undersigned residents, business owners, and community members, write to express our strong support
for the Alameda Lanes Repurposing Project. This initiative represents thoughtful, evidence-based
transportation planning that will improve safety, enhance livability, and benefit our community for years to come.
Why We Support This Project
Enhanced Safety for All Users: The project's safety improvements including protected crossings, pedestrian
islands, and reduced crossing distances will significantly improve safety for pedestrians, cyclists, and drivers
alike. These evidence-based design elements have proven successful in cities across the nation.
Improved Traffic Flow: Dedicated turn lanes will prevent turning vehicles from blocking through traffic, actually
improving traffic flow and reducing congestion. This is a win-win solution that addresses mobility while
prioritizing safety.
Neighborhood Protection: Strategic bollards and turn restrictions will prevent dangerous cut-through traffic in
residential areas, making our neighborhoods safer for families and children. This protects the residential
character of our community.
Economic Benefits: Research consistently shows that walkable streets with appropriate traffic calming
measures increase local business revenue. Slower, safer streets encourage people to stop, shop, and engage
with local businesses.
Environmental Responsibility: By creating more walkable infrastructure and encouraging sustainable
transportation options, this project aligns with Denver's climate goals and contributes to improved air quality for
our community.
Addressing Concerns with Facts
We understand that some community members have expressed concerns about this project. However,
extensive research and real-world examples demonstrate that well-designed road reconfigurations like this one:
Typically have minimal impact on travel times while dramatically improving safety Reduce serious crashes by
up to 25% according to Federal Highway Administration studies
Improve pedestrian safety by up to 40% with protected crossing infrastructure Support local economic
development through increased walkability Maintain emergency vehicle access while improving response times
through reduced congestion
Our Request
We urge DOTI to move forward with the Alameda Lanes Repurposing Project as planned. This project
represents the kind of forward-thinking, evidence-based transportation planning that Denver needs to create
safer, more livable neighborhoods for all residents.
The safety of our community members—pedestrians, cyclists, and drivers —should be our top priority. This
project achieves that goal while maintaining mobility and supporting local economic development.
Thank you for your leadership in prioritizing safety and livability in our transportation infrastructure.

182 signatures - Community Letter of Support for the Dave Hawkins iamdavehawkins@gmail.com mike.johnston@denvergov.org; amy.ford@denvergov.org; gregory.cieciek@denvergov.org; flor.alvidrez@denvergov.org Dear Mayor Johnston, Ms. Ford, and DOTI Representatives:
Preface: This letter represents an organic grassroots effort organized by David Hawkins, a local father
concerned about traffic safety in our neighborhood, neighbors, and community members. The signatures were
collected through our community website safer-alameda.com<http://safer-alameda.com>, where neighbors
could learn about the project and add their support. Over several weeks we have gathered 182 signatures from
community members who support this important safety initiative.
We, the undersigned residents, business owners, and community members, write to express our strong support
for the Alameda Lanes Repurposing Project. This initiative represents thoughtful, evidence-based
transportation planning that will improve safety, enhance livability, and benefit our community for years to come.
Why We Support This Project
Enhanced Safety for All Users: The project's safety improvements including protected crossings, pedestrian
islands, and reduced crossing distances will significantly improve safety for pedestrians, cyclists, and drivers
alike. These evidence-based design elements have proven successful in cities across the nation.
Improved Traffic Flow: Dedicated turn lanes will prevent turning vehicles from blocking through traffic, actually
improving traffic flow and reducing congestion. This is a win-win solution that addresses mobility while
prioritizing safety.
Neighborhood Protection: Strategic bollards and turn restrictions will prevent dangerous cut-through traffic in
residential areas, making our neighborhoods safer for families and children. This protects the residential
character of our community.
Economic Benefits: Research consistently shows that walkable streets with appropriate traffic calming
measures increase local business revenue. Slower, safer streets encourage people to stop, shop, and engage
with local businesses.
Environmental Responsibility: By creating more walkable infrastructure and encouraging sustainable
transportation options, this project aligns with Denver's climate goals and contributes to improved air quality for
our community.
Addressing Concerns with Facts
We understand that some community members have expressed concerns about this project. However,
extensive research and real-world examples demonstrate that well-designed road reconfigurations like this one:
Typically have minimal impact on travel times while dramatically improving safety Reduce serious crashes by
up to 25% according to Federal Highway Administration studies
Improve pedestrian safety by up to 40% with protected crossing infrastructure Support local economic
development through increased walkability Maintain emergency vehicle access while improving response times
through reduced congestion
Our Request
We urge DOTI to move forward with the Alameda Lanes Repurposing Project as planned. This project
represents the kind of forward-thinking, evidence-based transportation planning that Denver needs to create
safer, more livable neighborhoods for all residents.
The safety of our community members—pedestrians, cyclists, and drivers —should be our top priority. This
project achieves that goal while maintaining mobility and supporting local economic development.
Thank you for your leadership in prioritizing safety and livability in our transportation infrastructure.
Sincerely, David Hawkins & the following

<Flor.Alvidrez@denvergov.org> Subject: [EXTERNAL] 182 signatures - Community Letter of Support for the
Alameda Lanes Repurposing Project
Dear Mayor Johnston, Ms. Ford, and DOTI Representatives:
Preface: This letter represents an organic grassroots effort organized by David Hawkins, a local father
concerned about traffic safety in our neighborhood, neighbors, and community members. The signatures were
collected through our community website safer-alameda.com<http:/safer-alameda.com>, where neighbors
could learn about the project and add their support. Over several weeks we have gathered 182 signatures from
community members who support this important safety initiative.
We, the undersigned residents, business owners, and community members, write to express our strong support
for the Alameda Lanes Repurposing Project. This initiative represents thoughtful, evidence-based
transportation planning that will improve safety, enhance livability, and benefit our community for years to come.
Why We Support This Project
Enhanced Safety for All Users: The project's safety improvements including protected crossings, pedestrian
islands, and reduced crossing distances will significantly improve safety for pedestrians, cyclists, and drivers
alike. These evidence-based design elements have proven successful in cities across the nation.
Improved Traffic Flow: Dedicated turn lanes will prevent turning vehicles from blocking through traffic, actually
improving traffic flow and reducing congestion. This is a win-win solution that addresses mobility while
prioritizing safety.
Neighborhood Protection: Strategic bollards and turn restrictions will prevent dangerous cut-through traffic in
residential areas, making our neighborhoods safer for families and children. This protects the residential
character of our community.
Economic Benefits: Research consistently shows that walkable streets with appropriate traffic calming
measures increase local business revenue. Slower, safer streets encourage people to stop, shop, and engage
with local businesses.
Environmental Responsibility: By creating more walkable infrastructure and encouraging sustainable
transportation options, this project aligns with Denver's climate goals and contributes to improved air quality for
our community.
Addressing Concerns with Facts
We understand that some community members have expressed concerns about this project. However,
extensive research and real-world examples demonstrate that well-designed road reconfigurations like this one:
Typically have minimal impact on travel times while dramatically improving safety Reduce serious crashes by
up to 25% according to Federal Highway Administration studies
Improve pedestrian safety by up to 40% with protected crossing infrastructure Support local economic
development through increased walkability Maintain emergency vehicle access while improving response times
through reduced congestion
Our Request
We urge DOTI to move forward with the Alameda Lanes Repurposing Project as planned. This project
represents the kind of forward-thinking, evidence-based transportation planning that Denver needs to create
safer, more livable neighborhoods for all residents.
The safety of our community members—pedestrians, cyclists, and drivers —should be our top priority. This
project achieves that goal while maintaining mobility and supporting local economic development.
Thank you for your leadership in prioritizing safety and livability in our transportation infrastructure.
Sincerely, David Hawkins & the following

From: Dave Hawkins <iamdavehawkins@gmail.com> Sent: Monday, August 25, 2025 2:17 PM To: Johnston,
Mike - MO Mayor <Mike.Johnston@denvergov.org>; Ford, Amy - DOTI HE3258 Manager Department of
Transportation and Infrastructure <Amy.Ford@denvergov.org>; Cieciek, Gregory M. - DOTI Project Manager
Engineering Senior <Gregory.Cieciek@denvergov.org>; Lanphier, Molly - DOTI Project Manager Engineering
Senior <Molly.Lanphier@denvergov.org> Cc: Alvidrez, Flor C. - CC Member District 7 Denver City Council
<Flor.Alvidrez@denvergov.org> Subject: [EXTERNAL] 182 signatures - Community Letter of Support for the
Alameda Lanes Repurposing Project
Dear Mayor Johnston, Ms. Ford, and DOTI Representatives:
Preface: This letter represents an organic grassroots effort organized by David Hawkins, a local father
concerned about traffic safety in our neighborhood, neighbors, and community members. The signatures were
collected through our community website safer-alameda.com<http:/safer-alameda.com>, where neighbors
could learn about the project and add their support. Over several weeks we have gathered 182 signatures from
community members who support this important safety initiative.
We, the undersigned residents, business owners, and community members, write to express our strong support
for the Alameda Lanes Repurposing Project. This initiative represents thoughtful, evidence-based
transportation planning that will improve safety, enhance livability, and benefit our community for years to come.
Why We Support This Project
Enhanced Safety for All Users: The project's safety improvements including protected crossings, pedestrian
islands, and reduced crossing distances will significantly improve safety for pedestrians, cyclists, and drivers
alike. These evidence-based design elements have proven successful in cities across the nation.
Improved Traffic Flow: Dedicated turn lanes will prevent turning vehicles from blocking through traffic, actually
improving traffic flow and reducing congestion. This is a win-win solution that addresses mobility while
prioritizing safety.
Neighborhood Protection: Strategic bollards and turn restrictions will prevent dangerous cut-through traffic in
residential areas, making our neighborhoods safer for families and children. This protects the residential
character of our community.
Economic Benefits: Research consistently shows that walkable streets with appropriate traffic calming
measures increase local business revenue. Slower, safer streets encourage people to stop, shop, and engage
with local businesses.
Environmental Responsibility: By creating more walkable infrastructure and encouraging sustainable
transportation options, this project aligns with Denver's climate goals and contributes to improved air quality for
our community.
Addressing Concerns with Facts
We understand that some community members have expressed concerns about this project. However,
extensive research and real-world examples demonstrate that well-designed road reconfigurations like this one:
Typically have minimal impact on travel times while dramatically improving safety Reduce serious crashes by
up to 25% according to Federal Highway Administration studies
Improve pedestrian safety by up to 40% with protected crossing infrastructure Support local economic
development through increased walkability Maintain emergency vehicle access while improving response times
through reduced congestion
Our Request
We urge DOTI to move forward with the Alameda Lanes Repurposing Project as planned. This project
represents the kind of forward-thinking, evidence-based transportation planning that Denver needs to create
safer, more livable neighborhoods for all residents.
The safety of our community members—pedestrians, cyclists, and drivers —should be our top priority. This
project achieves that goal while maintaining mobility and supporting local economic development.
Thank you for your leadership in prioritizing safety and livability in our transportation infrastructure.
Sincerely, David Hawkins & the following

b:
Get Outlook for iOS<https://aka.ms/o0ukef> ________________________________ From: K.Dieter Heidrich
<dieterandmarie1@comcast.net> Sent: Wednesday, August 27, 2025 2:25:45 PM To: Ford, Amy - DOTI
HE3258 Manager Department of Transportation and Infrastructure <Amy.Ford@denvergov.org> Subject:
[EXTERNAL] Alameda Project
Klaus D & Marie K Heidrich 480 S Marion Pkwy Denver, CO. 80209
August 27, 2025
Ms Amy Ford amy.Ford@denver.org<mailto:amy.Ford@denver.org>
Dear Ms Ford, It has come to our attention that major changes are planned for Alameda Av. between S.
Franklin and S. Grant. We live on the intersection of Marion Parkway and Virginia, so this project is right in “our
backyard”.
We have lived here for going on 13 years and have seen the traffic growth on Alameda. While we agree that
adding a dedicated left turn lane is a good idea but to accomplish this by losing one entire lane of traffic is the
wrong answer. Yes, there currently is a very infrequent occasional backup in the left lane if a car is trying to turn
left, crossing the opposing traffic. Forcing all traffic, especially the morning and evening rush hour into one lane
will cause major backups in this one remaining through lane for the entire length of the planned project.
Since there are no four lane East West through streets in this area, it will force frustrated drivers to available
side streets. The most desirable will become Virginia, a relatively quiet two lane street right by Wash Park,
which definitely is not prepared to serve as an Alameda overflow.
We implore you to ask your staff to rethink this drastic solution for a very minor problem.
Best regards,
Dieter and Marie

Klaus D & Marie K Heidrich 480 S Marion Pkwy Denver, CO. 80209

Dear Molly Lanphier DOTI Liaison for Council DIstrict 6
ActforAlameda’s Key Concerns lists many issues that truly need to be addressed before this poorly planned
lane repurposing project takes place, hopefully never.
The following are some added thoughts from a Wash Park East resident who lives in the Park Lane
Condominiums.
It will be somewhat similar to Downing south from I25 to Evans but narrower & much more intrusive to the
neighborhood.
No new sidewalks or curbs.
No recent traffic studies that include the redevelopment of the old Bed, Bath & Beyond site at University & 1st
Av with possible 1000 apartments & new businesses.
No discussion of the need for 4way stop signs at EACH intersection in the neighborhoods that will see
increased traffic as cars back up in the 1 lane each way on Alameda & divert to smaller neighborhood side
streets.
Could find no discussion of additional safety for Steele School, the block Alameda to Dakota, Marion Pkwy to
Lafayette.
Marion Pkwy is now a raceway for bicycles, motorcycles, construction trucks, cars, garbage trucks. Fire trucks
are the only safe vehicles using Marion Pkwy.
Virginia Av & Dakota Av are narrow 2way 1 lane in each direction streets with parking on both sides that cannot
handle the additional traffic that will avoid Alameda.
Additional noise & pollution in a premier, classic Denver neighborhood.
Cynthia Massirio 460 S Marion Pkwy 503C 80209 303 437-0492

Hello, I live at 1101 E Alameda Ave. I am I in support of the Alameda lane repurposing project. My daughter’s
friend was hit 2 years ago walking to Steele Elementary. Another Neighbors kid was hit coming home from
middle school. We are mere inches away from cars going 50 mph. Please don’t cave to a few rich people that
on my have 2 lanes of traffic and an ROW over in East Wash Park. Please see the attached photos of how this
project begun- when a car drove into my neighbor’s house (they were sober). Please call me if you want to chat.

b:
Get Outlook for iOS<https://aka.ms/o0ukef> ________________________________ From: Nancy Picucci
<nlantis@gmail.com> Sent: Thursday, August 28, 2025 4:25:16 PM To: Ford, Amy - DOTI HE3258 Manager
Department of Transportation and Infrastructure <Amy.Ford@denvergov.org> Subject: [EXTERNAL] Alameda
Project
Good day-
I have been thrilled to hear that Alameda Lane Repurposing Project was a GO. That street in that 12 block
stretch has a lot of pedestrian traffic and many children that must cross it to attend nearby public schools and
walk to friend’s homes. A reduced speed limit is key. While I am not usually a proponent of bike lanes, I think
this plan makes sense due to the children in the neighborhood getting themselves to and from school and the
number of people in the community that commute to and from work via bikes. People currently drive 45+ mph
down Alameda and Downing when possible. During school hours people shoot through stop lights and stop
signs endangering pedestrians. Reducing the lanes to 2 will increase visibility for cars and pedestrians. Too
many people have been injured and died already. Please work to continue the Alameda Project to keep our
communities safe and increasing the walkability of our neighborhood.
Many thanks, Nancy Picucci 303.356.0598

Hello, I am writing in support of the proposed Alameda Ave changes to slow traffic and make it more safe for
pedestrians. I was around for the last debacle and know the success of this plan is not guaranteed but, people
are driving faster these days in heavier vehicles, particularly pickup trucks. The police seem to have vanished
so people feel free to speed and weave.
This process has been ongoing for years and years. There have been numerous notices that were seen by
neighbors who take the time to read.
Alameda has been a struggle for the city and state for decades, especially since 1936 when a flood along
Cherry Creek took out the Alameda bridge crossing the creek that obviously was never rebuilt. Perhaps it
wasn't due to it being during the Great Depression but, given my experience working with neighborhoods, I
suspect that the wealthier residents, such as the earlier Polo Club members, have been very glad, and vocal,
against changes that are perceived as less attractive for them, even tho this is a state road and wasn't designed
as a residential street.
Let's hope this plan works. Something must be done.
Sarah O. McCarthy c: 303.204.8722
x:
Alameda Ave project Sarah McCarthy somccarthy@gmail.com amy.ford@denvergov.org Hello, I am writing in support of the proposed Alameda Ave changes to slow traffic and make it more safe for
pedestrians. I was around for the last debacle and know the success of this plan is not guaranteed but, people
are driving faster these days in heavier vehicles, particularly pickup trucks. The police seem to have vanished
so people feel free to speed and weave.
This process has been ongoing for years and years. There have been numerous notices that were seen by
neighbors who take the time to read.
Alameda has been a struggle for the city and state for decades, especially since 1936 when a flood along
Cherry Creek took out the Alameda bridge crossing the creek that obviously was never rebuilt. Perhaps it
wasn't due to it being during the Great Depression but, given my experience working with neighborhoods, I
suspect that the wealthier residents, such as the earlier Polo Club members, have been very glad, and vocal,
against changes that are perceived as less attractive for them, even tho this is a state road and wasn't designed
as a residential street.
Let's hope this plan works. Something must be done.
Sarah O. McCarthy c: 303.204.8722

b:
Hi Matt,
I wanted to follow up about the traffic signal modification request from my commented SOW. I did a review
yesterday to identify the signalized intersection with permitted left turns in the eastbound direction along
Alameda. Our main concern is that the signal mast arm may not be long enough to properly align the signal
head above the lane. I identified only one location of major concern - Emerson. I am hoping you and your team
can review this assumption, and also do your own exploration to determine where we might need to modify the
signal heads to properly control movements in the eastbound and westbound directions.
I do have a call tomorrow morning and am hoping you can provide me the draft scope and fee proposal by 9am
tomorrow morning. Let me know if that won't be possible - thanks again.
steven
steven
Steven Marfitano, PE | Engineer City & County of Denver Department of Transportation & Infrastructure |
Transportation Implementation Division

cc: "Boncore; Brett - DOTI CE0403 Engineer-Architect Supervisor" <Brett.Boncore@denvergov.org>; "Pratt;
b:
Hi, Steven. Thanks for the email. I’m realizing now that I never sent the invitation for us to connect following our
correspondence about a meeting time this week, my apologies. I’ll send now for 2P, assuming that time still
works for you.
The signal modifications are the largest outstanding item in our approach. Please see a few notes below
regarding an initial review of the intersections. I’d like to confirm the City’s desired approach for the
design/construction of modified signal structures on these corners or if signal head modification is the desired
approach. This will obviously impact the design and construction costs.
* Logan – Signal modification/reconstruction not necessary. * Pearl – Mast arm extension on SEC is needed to
align with center of future EBLT. Arm currently only extends to the center of the road. * Washington – Minor
signal equipment adjustments may be necessary. * Emerson – Mast arm extension on SEC is needed to align
with center of future EBLT. Arm currently only extends to the center of the road. * Downing – Minor signal
equipment adjustments may be necessary. * Marion – Minor signal equipment adjustments may be necessary.
I was also hoping to discuss the Virginia & Downing intersection with you in more detail. Pending analysis and
recommendation, there may be signal modifications at this intersection, but they are likely limited to minor
equipment modifications. More importantly, you mentioned the potential of curb extensions. From a scoping
perspective, I’m trying to determine if we should budget for this condition which would be our worst-case
scenario level of effort. Furthermore, we would need to consider effort for design survey and storm
modifications. I’d like to get your input on how to approach at this stage of the project.
Regarding the status of a draft agreement; we are on track to have a draft developed by EOD with the
exception of the items noted above. Let’s connect later to discuss these items. Thanks

Hi Matt,
Thanks for the time this afternoon, here are a couple more answers as you refine the scope/fee.
* Pearl - please assume that the existing signal pole will be maintained and either a) the arm will be replaced
after confirmation that the footing/design is adequate or b) the left turn head will be added onto the existing arm
(following your current design) <-- Bullet added to the Project Understanding section of the Agreement
confirming approach to signal improvements.
* Emerson - I have confirmed that we would like to include replacement of the signal pole for the eastbound
movements. Additionally, we would like the design to include upgraded controller cabinet and ADA compliant
ramps (reconstruction of all elements on that corner). <-- I have added a very high level approach to this signal
design in Add Alternate Task 7. Due to the time constraints of this change, I won’t be able to provide a full
scope and fee at this time; but have rather noted this as “TBD”. I know this isn’t ideal; however, based on my
understanding of the overall situation from our call yesterday, it seems like this can be acceptable for your call
today. Please see below for a few considerations that will impact the scope/fee that will need to be confirmed
prior to finalizing.
* Interconnect – I am emailing Josh Jones to confirm what the existing interconnect is along the corridor, if any.
We will need to plan to connect this into the revised cabinet location if present. * Survey – I’ll need to get a
quote for this data collection at the intersection. * Power – It doesn’t appear that there is an existing pedestal
service at the intersection based on Google StreetView. I’ll need to swing by later to confirm. If this doesn’t
exist, the City typically requires that this be included with modification/construction. Would the City like us to
include scope for an electrical one-line diagram and coordination with the service provider (Xcel)? * Drainage –
There is an existing inlet on the SEC (Google StreetView <https://www.google.com/maps/@39.7110705,-104.9
769204,3a,75y,111.2h,90.97t/data=!3m7!1e1!3m5!1sK0Lx2RV39Y8mrNn_hkpE4g!2e0!6shttps:*2F*2Fstreetvie

4!8i8192?entry=ttu&g_ep=EgoyMDI1MDkxNC4wIKXMDSoASAFQAw*3D*3D> ). Elsewhere along the corridor,
existing inlets have been maintained with reconstruction activities around them. I’m assuming that we want to
