Coordination
Hi Matt,
Thanks for the time this afternoon, here are a couple more answers as you refine the scope/fee.
* Pearl - please assume that the existing signal pole will be maintained and either a) the arm will be replaced
after confirmation that the footing/design is adequate or b) the left turn head will be added onto the existing arm
(following your current design) <-- Bullet added to the Project Understanding section of the Agreement
confirming approach to signal improvements.
* Emerson - I have confirmed that we would like to include replacement of the signal pole for the eastbound
movements. Additionally, we would like the design to include upgraded controller cabinet and ADA compliant
ramps (reconstruction of all elements on that corner). <-- I have added a very high level approach to this signal
design in Add Alternate Task 7. Due to the time constraints of this change, I won’t be able to provide a full
scope and fee at this time; but have rather noted this as “TBD”. I know this isn’t ideal; however, based on my
understanding of the overall situation from our call yesterday, it seems like this can be acceptable for your call
today. Please see below for a few considerations that will impact the scope/fee that will need to be confirmed
prior to finalizing.
* Interconnect – I am emailing Josh Jones to confirm what the existing interconnect is along the corridor, if any.
We will need to plan to connect this into the revised cabinet location if present. * Survey – I’ll need to get a
quote for this data collection at the intersection. * Power – It doesn’t appear that there is an existing pedestal
service at the intersection based on Google StreetView. I’ll need to swing by later to confirm. If this doesn’t
exist, the City typically requires that this be included with modification/construction. Would the City like us to
include scope for an electrical one-line diagram and coordination with the service provider (Xcel)? * Drainage –
There is an existing inlet on the SEC (Google StreetView <https://www.google.com/maps/@39.7110705,-104.9
769204,3a,75y,111.2h,90.97t/data=!3m7!1e1!3m5!1sK0Lx2RV39Y8mrNn_hkpE4g!2e0!6shttps:*2F*2Fstreetvie
wpixels-pa.googleapis.com*2Fv1*2Fthumbnail*3Fcb_client*3Dmaps_sv.tactile*26w*3D900*26h*3D600*26pitch
*3D-0.9708904989167593*26panoid*3DK0Lx2RV39Y8mrNn_hkpE4g*26yaw*3D111.19549616723357!7i1638
4!8i8192?entry=ttu&g_ep=EgoyMDI1MDkxNC4wIKXMDSoASAFQAw*3D*3D> ). Elsewhere along the corridor,
existing inlets have been maintained with reconstruction activities around them. I’m assuming that we want to
take the same approach at this intersection. We will plan to not include any drainage improvements or analysis.
Please include the Emerson items as a standalone scope item as we determine funding for the improvements.
Thanks again,
steven

this stage of the project, please see below.
* SUE – The signal modification improvements are not anticipated to require SUE analysis, especially if we are
maintaining existing conduit and not completing a new bore shot below the roadways. We will plan to complete
standard utility locates (811) with our survey unless discussed otherwise. * Wiring – Because the cabinet is
being relocated, the entire intersection will likely need to be re-wired. This will not impact our design fees, but
will be another consideration for construction costs.
Thanks

cc: Gormley, Mary <mary.gormley@kimley-horn.com>; Pratt, Anthony <anthony.pratt@kimley-horn.com>
b:
Hi Matt, thanks for the time to connect - here's the analysis I had started - please focus on the bulleted changes
(not on the fee, which are obviously just my assumptions). Def call or write if there's any questions. I will let you
know how my conversation with Brett goes on Monday.
Here was the original fee: [cid:18e639ad-8b83-4984-85f4-50a632602cfb] The original schedule lasted 9
months with the first 3-4 months representing the data collection and analysis.
The goal here is to identify the essential deliverables for a MVP; which means preserving the design for
Alameda, signal timings for the corridor, and PI support. Here are the scope items we will need to cut to get
towards $50k.
1. Remove all data collection and majority traffic safety analysis and evaluation This includes removal of all
traffic counts, delay analysis, diversion analysis, KHA review and validation of the safety assessment, and
memo/documentation. The only item preserved from this task would be development of future signal timings,
and field observation and verification of timing upon implementation. This will mean we cannot answer queuing
specific questions from Amy Ford's email on 9/3. (these changes should reduce the schedule by approx 3
months, reducing task 1 by 33%; eliminate all data collection; and reduce traffic eval by 80%).
2. In order to be able to answer questions about diversion, I have already talked to Rinal about using the travel
demand model to make some measure of this by using the DRCOG travel demand model. I will need support
from the team to prioritize this effort so that we can give a high-level diversion answer to the public. We will not
incorporate this information into further operational analysis such as queuing. (If this is not preferred/possible,
we could make a very crude assumption that because only half the corridor capacity reduction is planned only
those volumes would be impacted - traffic counts show approximately 45% of traffic moves WB so we could
assume 4.5% diversion. This is not preferred, because congestion induced diversion is not linear with volume
and this would likely over-estimate our actual diversion impacts.)
1. Scaled back public engagement support - given the removal of the traffic evaluation, materials updated
provided by KHA will include translation and materials demonstrating the project components. We will use the
materials prepared for the Aug Amy Ford meeting as a basis for the public information and may ask KHA to
provide graphical support. (assume 60% reduction)
2. Transfer Downing/Virginia In-house - many of the alternatives identified with Eric are easily accomplished in
house with TME support and implemented by maintenance. Further discussion is needed to determine which
components might be necessarily done by consultant and if the installation would be maintenance or
construction. (assumes $9k design cost reduction (17%))
1. Preserve all signal modification design as these are essential to the safety performance of the corridor. May
explore only signal pole replacement at Emerson by Ops, but design would be provided by KHA in case
construction dollars can be found for the full pole, cabinet, and sidewalk work. At Pearl, would include bolted-on
mast arm replacement knowing that if construction dollars are not available, the existing mast arm could be
used.
2. Removal of one review stage. Given the simplification of the overall scope of work (initially included 60%,
90%, 100%, and Last Call) and the fact this plan set has already been all the way through our review process;
propose removal of the 60% plan set and review. (assumes $9k design cost reduction (17%)) [Matt - this was
my thinking before you proposed a redlined plan set, which I think we should continue to explore so we
preserve budget capacity for Task 7.)

Hi Matt, thanks for the time to connect - here's the analysis I had started - please focus on the bulleted changes
(not on the fee, which are obviously just my assumptions). Def call or write if there's any questions. I will let you
know how my conversation with Brett goes on Monday.
Here was the original fee: [cid:18e639ad-8b83-4984-85f4-50a632602cfb] The original schedule lasted 9
months with the first 3-4 months representing the data collection and analysis.
The goal here is to identify the essential deliverables for a MVP; which means preserving the design for
Alameda, signal timings for the corridor, and PI support. Here are the scope items we will need to cut to get
towards $50k.
1. Remove all data collection and majority traffic safety analysis and evaluation This includes removal of all
traffic counts, delay analysis, diversion analysis, KHA review and validation of the safety assessment, and
memo/documentation. The only item preserved from this task would be development of future signal timings,
and field observation and verification of timing upon implementation. This will mean we cannot answer queuing
specific questions from Amy Ford's email on 9/3. (these changes should reduce the schedule by approx 3
months, reducing task 1 by 33%; eliminate all data collection; and reduce traffic eval by 80%).
2. In order to be able to answer questions about diversion, I have already talked to Rinal about using the travel
demand model to make some measure of this by using the DRCOG travel demand model. I will need support
from the team to prioritize this effort so that we can give a high-level diversion answer to the public. We will not
incorporate this information into further operational analysis such as queuing. (If this is not preferred/possible,
we could make a very crude assumption that because only half the corridor capacity reduction is planned only
those volumes would be impacted - traffic counts show approximately 45% of traffic moves WB so we could
assume 4.5% diversion. This is not preferred, because congestion induced diversion is not linear with volume
and this would likely over-estimate our actual diversion impacts.)
1. Scaled back public engagement support - given the removal of the traffic evaluation, materials updated
provided by KHA will include translation and materials demonstrating the project components. We will use the
materials prepared for the Aug Amy Ford meeting as a basis for the public information and may ask KHA to
provide graphical support. (assume 60% reduction)
2. Transfer Downing/Virginia In-house - many of the alternatives identified with Eric are easily accomplished in
house with TME support and implemented by maintenance. Further discussion is needed to determine which
components might be necessarily done by consultant and if the installation would be maintenance or
construction. (assumes $9k design cost reduction (17%))
1. Preserve all signal modification design as these are essential to the safety performance of the corridor. May
explore only signal pole replacement at Emerson by Ops, but design would be provided by KHA in case
construction dollars can be found for the full pole, cabinet, and sidewalk work. At Pearl, would include bolted-on
mast arm replacement knowing that if construction dollars are not available, the existing mast arm could be
used.
2. Removal of one review stage. Given the simplification of the overall scope of work (initially included 60%,
90%, 100%, and Last Call) and the fact this plan set has already been all the way through our review process;
propose removal of the 60% plan set and review. (assumes $9k design cost reduction (17%)) [Matt - this was
my thinking before you proposed a redlined plan set, which I think we should continue to explore so we
preserve budget capacity for Task 7.)

cc: "Gormley; Mary" <Mary.Gormley@kimley-horn.com>; "Pratt; Anthony" <Anthony.Pratt@kimley-horn.com>
b:
Hi, Steven
I wanted to reach out with an updated version of the draft agreement for your review. This agreement has been
updated based on your direction provided. Let me know if you have any further comments or questions.
I wanted to note two items:
* SUE – Currently noted as an exclusion. Based on the email from Josh Jones regarding the fiber interconnect
(attached for reference), we will likely need to provide a new bored conduit from the proposed controller to the
splice location on the NEC. This may warrant SUE. I’m connecting with our lead today to confirm based on the
requirements of SB 18-167. * Review – The document still includes a draft watermark and does not have a
signature. I have to route this for internal review with our contract specialist team. I am emailing them now and
requesting an expedited review. I’m hopeful it can be completed today.
Can you help me track down any available record drawings for the traffic signal at Alameda Ave & Emerson St?
In the past I’ve requested these from Fadia Sawaqed. I am happy to do so if this is still the best option. Can you
let me know?
Lastly, I’m not sure that we talked about it explicitly, I am out tomorrow. I can be online tomorrow morning
potentially, but I’d like to get this wrapped up today if possible. I’ll let you know when I have updates on the
items noted above.
Thanks!

f: 2025-10-10_13-16-02 - _EXTERNAL_ Alameda Lane Reduction - Downing Turning Movement.pdf
s: Alameda Lane Reduction - Downing Turning Movements
cc: "Gormley; Mary" <Mary.Gormley@kimley-horn.com>; "Pratt; Anthony" <Anthony.Pratt@kimley-horn.com>
b:
Hey, Steven. I connected with the team and had them take a (quick) look back to investigate the decision
regarding the turning movements. Please find a few notes below regarding the Downing intersection based on
our review of past files and correspondence.
In our alternatives analysis we did not introduce the EB or WB left turn movements at Downing Street. We did
complete an intersection analysis in Synchro with these left turn movements being allowed but we did not go
through with this configuration with our preferred alternative. We weren’t readily able to find formal
reasoning/justification in our documents. We believe that the introduction of the left turn lanes was not
supported by the City based on the following:
* This location has the 2nd highest number of crashes along the mainline. * Keep the existing conditions with no
left turn movements allowed * The travel time along the corridor will increase if operations are protected
permitted by adding another phase to the signal operations * The pedestrian safety is exposed on the north and
south crosswalks if left turn movements are introduced
Regardless, we can discuss this in more detail when the project gets going again.
Thanks for your consideration. Have a great weekend!
Matt Ciarkowski, P.E. (CO, TX, WA, WY, NE) Kimley-Horn | 1125 17th Street, Suite 1400, Denver, CO 80202
Direct: 720.943.5660 | Mobile: 817.308.7306 Celebrating 18 years as one of FORTUNE’s 100 Best Companies
to Work For<http://fortune.com/best-companies/kimley-horn-and-associates-7/>
Upcoming Out-of-Office Dates: 10/13, 11/14

complete an intersection analysis in Synchro with these left turn movements being allowed but we did not go
through with this configuration with our preferred alternative. We weren’t readily able to find formal
reasoning/justification in our documents. We believe that the introduction of the left turn lanes was not
supported by the City based on the following:
* This location has the 2nd highest number of crashes along the mainline. * Keep the existing conditions with no
left turn movements allowed * The travel time along the corridor will increase if operations are protected
permitted by adding another phase to the signal operations * The pedestrian safety is exposed on the north and
south crosswalks if left turn movements are introduced
Regardless, we can discuss this in more detail when the project gets going again.
Thanks for your consideration. Have a great weekend!
Matt Ciarkowski, P.E. (CO, TX, WA, WY, NE) Kimley-Horn | 1125 17th Street, Suite 1400, Denver, CO 80202

b:
Hi Matt and Bryanna -
We got questions back about your invoice:
"I'm not able to verify the rates/roles with a red X on page 1. Can you share with me where I can find these, or
have the vendor revise the invoice. Also, Analyst II has 2 different rates for the same role.. Apologies if I'm

f: 2025-10-31_13-16-17 - _EXTERNAL_ RE_ Alameda Lane Reduction - Time Extension (19).pdf
s: RE: Alameda Lane Reduction - Time Extension
b:
Hi, Steven
The Baseline proposal provided by the subconsultant does not include hourly breakdown, rates, or
classifications. I can request this information from them at this time. Baseline was not included/approved as a
part of the original on-call, so we will need to add them at a task order level. We have done this previously
through an add letter (example attached for reference).
We will complete a similar exercise for Mayo; however we will reference their services based on a unit/cost
breakdown, not hourly, given the nature of their work.
Please let me know if you have any questions or concerns with this approach. I’ll reach out to those subs now
and provide you with an update when it is available.
Thanks

f: 2025-11-06_16-43-55 - Follow Up on NS Movements along Alameda.pdf
s: Follow Up on N/S Movements along Alameda
cc: Williams, Nicholas - DOTI Deputy Manager Internal & External Affairs
b:
Hi Jason-
I just wanted to clarify Jill’s question regarding restricting the North South movements along Alameda. The short
answer is we will not be restricting movement, although there are proposed changes to the allowed turning
movements at intersections along Alameda. For example, we are proposing "right-in right-out", "three-quarter
movements", and "restricted lefts" at some locations. In all cases, two way travel will be allowed on every leg at
every intersection (save one-way streets of course), the allowable movements accessing may be restricted
however.
Hope that makes sense! Thanks for a productive call.
Best-
Molly

f: 2025-11-10_17-13-08 - _EXTERNAL_ RE_ Alameda Lane Reduction - Time Extension (2).pdf
s: RE: Alameda Lane Reduction - Time Extension
b:
Hi, Steven. Thanks for providing these comments. I haven’t been able to review in detail, but at first glance they
look manageable. I wanted to note a few items, please see below.
Survey Limits – I can reach out to Baseline to get a revised fee that includes 100’ from the intersection on the
east and south legs of the intersection. We originally included the entire intersection given some of the ultimate
uncertainty regarding the conduit boring by the City, separate from this agreement. Also, the cost of the
additional data pickup is usually nominal once they are in the field and I defer to getting a complete dataset for
unforeseen circumstances. I’ll let you know what I hear back.
Survey Fees – We originally got a fee estimate from our internal KH survey team that were higher than
expected so we reached out to Baseline who has historically been a quality provider who is also more
affordable. Their estimate was not significantly lower than our internal team, but we knew that budgets were
sensitive on this project and elected to move forward with them. Based on other conversations, it seems that
this may be where survey fees are heading and/or reflect the current market. I’m happy to consider and contact
an additional firm for an estimate, but want to be sensitive about the timing of the contract knowing it is sensitive
for the City. Would you like me to contact another firm or move forward with Baseline?
TCE – There is a retaining wall immediately adjacent to the back of sidewalk on the project corner (Google
Street View<https://www.google.com/maps/@39.7109747,-104.9769476,3a,48.9y,73.56h,86.25t/data=!3m7!1e
1!3m5!1sT6cm0PomeIB27q4Z_gdb5w!2e0!6shttps:*2F*2Fstreetviewpixels-pa.googleapis.com*2Fv1*2Fthumbn
ail*3Fcb_client*3Dmaps_sv.tactile*26w*3D900*26h*3D600*26pitch*3D3.753913632287876*26panoid*3DT6cm
0PomeIB27q4Z_gdb5w*26yaw*3D73.56351301562857!7i16384!8i8192?entry=ttu&g_ep=EgoyMDI1MTEwNS4
wIKXMDSoASAFQAw*3D*3D>). It also appears that the ROW is likely right at the back of walk/face of wall
based on the (approximate) parcel lines in Nearmap. That being said, I don’t anticipate that access to the
private property will be necessary. Let me know if you have any further notes on this that we need to account
for.
[cid:image002.png@01DC5265.47638030]
I’ll plan to get these to a stopping point and can finalize pending your feedback on Wednesday. Enjoy your time
out of office tomorrow!
Matt Ciarkowski, P.E. (CO, TX, WA, WY, NE) Kimley-Horn | 1125 17th Street, Suite 1400, Denver, CO 80202

f: 2025-11-11_16-58-14 - _EXTERNAL_ RE_ Alameda Lane Reduction - Time Extension (1).pdf
s: RE: Alameda Lane Reduction - Time Extension
b:
Hi, Steven. I hope you had a nice holiday. I wanted to follow up with an update on my email from yesterday. I’ve
completed an updated draft of the agreement based on the comments provided, please see attached. I’ve also
added a few notes in red to my email from yesterday below for additional information/context.
Let me know if you have any further comments or questions.
Thanks!
Matt Ciarkowski, P.E. (CO, TX, WA, WY, NE) Kimley-Horn | 1125 17th Street, Suite 1400, Denver, CO 80202
Direct: 720.943.5660 | Mobile: 817.308.7306 Celebrating 18 years as one of FORTUNE’s 100 Best Companies

From: Ciarkowski, Matt Sent: Monday, November 10, 2025 5:13 PM To: 'Marfitano, Steven C. - DOTI CE0397
Engineer' <Steven.Marfitano@denvergov.org> Subject: RE: Alameda Lane Reduction - Time Extension
Hi, Steven. Thanks for providing these comments. I haven’t been able to review in detail, but at first glance they
look manageable. I wanted to note a few items, please see below.
Survey Limits – I can reach out to Baseline to get a revised fee that includes 100’ from the intersection on the
east and south legs of the intersection. We originally included the entire intersection given some of the ultimate
uncertainty regarding the conduit boring by the City, separate from this agreement. Also, the cost of the
additional data pickup is usually nominal once they are in the field and I defer to getting a complete dataset for
unforeseen circumstances. I’ll let you know what I hear back. <-- Baseline confirmed that the project savings
would only be two hours of field mobilization (~$500) if we were to reduce the data collection limits to the SEC
only. I’d suggest we have them collect the entirety of the intersection in case something unforeseen comes up
during design.
Survey Fees – We originally got a fee estimate from our internal KH survey team that were higher than
expected so we reached out to Baseline who has historically been a quality provider who is also more
affordable. Their estimate was not significantly lower than our internal team, but we knew that budgets were
sensitive on this project and elected to move forward with them. Based on other conversations, it seems that
this may be where survey fees are heading and/or reflect the current market. I’m happy to consider and contact
an additional firm for an estimate, but want to be sensitive about the timing of the contract knowing it is sensitive
for the City. Would you like me to contact another firm or move forward with Baseline? <-- I haven’t yet reached
out to another firm for a quote. Please let me know if you would like me to.
TCE – There is a retaining wall immediately adjacent to the back of sidewalk on the project corner (Google
Street View<https://www.google.com/maps/@39.7109747,-104.9769476,3a,48.9y,73.56h,86.25t/data=!3m7!1e
1!3m5!1sT6cm0PomeIB27q4Z_gdb5w!2e0!6shttps:*2F*2Fstreetviewpixels-pa.googleapis.com*2Fv1*2Fthumbn
ail*3Fcb_client*3Dmaps_sv.tactile*26w*3D900*26h*3D600*26pitch*3D3.753913632287876*26panoid*3DT6cm
0PomeIB27q4Z_gdb5w*26yaw*3D73.56351301562857!7i16384!8i8192?entry=ttu&g_ep=EgoyMDI1MTEwNS4
wIKXMDSoASAFQAw*3D*3D>). It also appears that the ROW is likely right at the back of walk/face of wall
based on the (approximate) parcel lines in Nearmap. That being said, I don’t anticipate that access to the
private property will be necessary. Let me know if you have any further notes on this that we need to account
for. <-- No changes made to the agreement at this time.
[cid:image002.png@01DC5327.F0E56E30]
I’ll plan to get these to a stopping point and can finalize pending your feedback on Wednesday. Enjoy your time
out of office tomorrow!

f: 2025-11-12_09-12-38 - Re_ Alameda Lane Reduction - Time Extension.pdf
s: Re: Alameda Lane Reduction - Time Extension
b:
Thank you Matt, I will run these edits by the team.
* Agreed with your determination about maintaining the Baseline study area * No further action on survey costs
requested at this time * Thank you for the clear ROW acquisition language in the SOW.
I did notice you updated the fee table above the signature line and it looks like you transposed a couple of
numbers. Otherwise, I think I should be able to submit this later today.

From: Mark Schenberger <schenberger.1@gmail.com> Sent: Tuesday, November 11, 2025 9:11:31 PM To: Ford, Amy Subject: Re: [EXTERNAL] Watered down Alameda project
Hi Amy,
I appreciate the quick response. I understand we probably won't ever see eye to eye on this, but I feel the need to address some of the points you mention.
1. I find it hard to believe this timeline where DOTI was supposedly considering changes to the design all summer. My understanding is that you (or someone else at the department) told the DOTI advisory committee that the project was moving ahead as planned as of September or early October. Is that not true, or did something change after that?
2. Regarding your concern that the original design would send 500 more vehicles per day onto Virginia:
Virginia already sees 5000+ vehicles per day according to the DRCOG traffic counts site. I often bike along the street to get to Wash Park and I can't imagine that it going up to 5500 vehicles would make it feel noticeably different. I'd certainly take that trade-off to make Alameda much easier to cross.
In any case, if traffic diversion onto Virginia and other neighborhood streets was really the main concern with the original design, why not add modal filters to some of those streets to completely prevent the option of cutting through?
3. Putting RRFBs and painted crosswalks on a four lane road is just asking for tragedy. That just invites opportunities where a person walking ends up with a false sense of security when a car in the right lane stops, only for someone to speed around in the left lane. A friend of mine's child was killed while biking this year in exactly that scenario, so please don't try to convince me that the new design is prioritizing safety. Having one lane in each direction would be significantly safer for people crossing the road - end of story.
4. Whatever small amount of sidewalk buffer you're able to salvage in the new design will almost certainly have to be less than what you would have gotten originally - again making it less safe than it should have been.
I'm also curious if you would be willing to share the data on the robust number of folks you heard from this summer and fall? I'd be curious to see how that compares to the engagement you received during the public meetings and surveys back in 2024 that led to the safer design.
From: Ford, Amy Sent: Tuesday, November 11, 2025 6:46 PM Subject: RE: [EXTERNAL] Watered down Alameda project
Thank you again for your note and we appreciate frustration in the process. Over the summer we continued to take input and feedback from the public (and it was certainly a robust number of folks). We were aware of meetings where neighbors were together to discuss the corridor. Our engineers continued to evaluate the impact of the diversion of traffic on neighborhood streets - for instance around 500 more vehicles a day along Virginia.
We spent time evaluating how we could achieve our safety goals and address the safety concerns also that people had about the neighborhood streets. The left turns were important. The pedestrian crossings were important. And we are maintaining all of those improvements (and still looking at how we create the additional buffer from the sidewalks).
This was not process done in concert with anyone, and one that looked carefully at data, our safety goals and the feedback from the community. I appreciate the dialogue.
Amy
From: Mark Schenberger <schenberger.1@gmail.com> Sent: Tuesday, November 11, 2025 6:14 PM Subject: [EXTERNAL] Watered down Alameda project
Hi Amy,
I just wanted to reach out to say that I'm incredibly disappointed at your agency's decision to water down the planned improvements for Alameda Avenue.
As someone who lives directly adjacent to Alameda and has to cross or travel along on it nearly everyday, usually on foot or bike, I was very much looking forward to the planned redesign. Despite DOTI's attempts to defend the new proposal, the bottom line is that it's much less safe than the original. While the pedestrian islands and additional crossings are nice, what the project won't do is shorten the crossing distance for people walking, move cars further away from the narrow sidewalks, or measurably reduce speeding. As we all know, lowering the speed limit doesn't do a thing if the road remains wide.
Most of all, it's disappointing that the neighborhood was completely shut out of the the process as the new plan was developed. The original design involved a lengthy and open community engagement process, while this new design seems to have been drawn behind closed doors and in close concert with the "Act for Alameda" group/Jill Anschutz. Given the way this last minute reversal happened, it makes me wonder why I should bother engaging in the public process when it comes to other street safety projects.
-Mark

From: Boncore, Brett - DOTI CE0403 Engineer-Architect Supervisor <Brett.Boncore@denvergov.org> Sent: Thursday, July 24, 2025 10:21 AM To: Vigil, Rachael - DOTI CE0397 Engineer <Rachael.Vigil@denvergov.org>; Howell, Dallas - DOTI Project Manager Engineering Senior <Dallas.Howell@denvergov.org> Cc: Price, Brittany C. - DOTI CE2783 Engineer-Architect Director <Brittany.Price@denvergov.org> Subject: FW: Alameda project follow-up...

Hi Rachael,

There has been a lot of back and forth on how the Franklin/Alameda ped crossing improvement will be constructed. I had a chance to catch up with Brittany to settle this decision. The political/leadership perspective on this is that these improvements should move ahead in your project, separate from the Alameda Lane Reduction. The ped project is a huge priority of the council office and we don't want to gunk up that project with the ALR drama that is happening right now. So confirmed that you should keep it in your project. However, there are some minor additional redlines that we would like you to address on this sheet. I have attached my comments. Could you address these redlines for your meeting with Brittany on Friday?

@Howell, Dallas - I have redlined the ALR plan set to show the elements we will remove from our planset at this location, and saved to the Construction Bid Documents folder. Note: the RI/RO treatment will remain in the ALR plan set. Please see attached and let me know if you have any questions or preferences for how these redlines show.

I apologize for the confusion on this one... Please let me know if you have any questions. Thank you!

Brett Boncore, PE| Engineering Supervisor
